items.title: Database Transactions: A Comprehensive Guide with Go
items.author: Huseyn
items.content:
What is a Database Transaction?

A (often called a "TX") is a sequence of one or more database operations (e.g., INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) executed as a single logical unit. Transactions ensure data integrity and consistency by guaranteeing that either operations succeed or are applied, maintaining the database in a consistent state.

Key Characteristics -

  • : All operations in a transaction are completed successfully, or none are applied (all-or-nothing).
  • : The database transitions from one valid state to another, adhering to all constraints (e.g., foreign keys, unique constraints).
  • : Transactions are executed independently, preventing interference from concurrent transactions.
  • : Committed transactions are permanently saved, even in the event of a system failure.

: Transferring $100 from Account A to Account B involves two operations:

  1. Deduct $100 from Account A.

These must be part of a single transaction to avoid inconsistencies (e.g., money deducted but not credited).

To ensure transactions are reliable, follow these steps:

    • Start with or in SQL or use the Go packageέΑβs method.
    • This marks the transactionέΑβs start, tracking all operations as a single unit.
    • Perform SQL queries within the transaction.
    • Changes are held in a temporary state until committed or rolled back.
    • Use in SQL or in Go to make changes permanent.
    • Ensures durability, saving changes to disk.
    • Use in SQL or in Go to undo changes if an error occurs.
    • Ensures atomicity, restoring the database to its pre-transaction state.
    • Use isolation levels (e.g., READ COMMITTED, SERIALIZABLE) to control how transactions interact.
    • In Go, set the isolation level when beginning a transaction (e.g., db.BeginTx(context.Background(), &sql.TxOptions{Isolation: sql.LevelSerializable})).
    • Implement robust error handling in Go to catch failures (e.g., constraint violations) and trigger rollbacks.

Database State in Transactions

The refers to the data at any given moment. Transactions ensure valid state transitions:

  • : The database is consistent, satisfying all constraints.
  • : During a transaction, changes are held in memory or logs (e.g., using write-ahead logging or MVCC) and are not visible to other transactions until committed (depending on isolation level).
  • : On , changes are written to permanent storage, creating a new consistent state.
  • : On , changes are discarded, and the database reverts to its initial state.
  • : Isolation levels and mechanisms like MVCC (in PostgreSQL) ensure concurrent transactions see consistent snapshots.

What Happens if a Transaction Fails?

Failures can occur due to errors, crashes, or concurrency issues. The DBMS and application handle these as follows:

  • : If a query fails (e.g., due to a constraint violation), the transaction is rolled back, undoing all changes.
  • : Write-ahead logging ensures committed changes are recovered, and uncommitted changes are discarded on restart.
  • : The DBMS detects deadlocks and aborts one transaction, requiring the application to retry.
  • : In Go, use error handling to detect failures and call .

Implementing Transactions in Go

GoέΑβs package provides robust support for transactions, compatible with databases like PostgreSQL, MySQL (InnoDB), and SQLite. Below is an example of a transaction in Go using PostgreSQL to transfer money between accounts.

Go Example: Bank Transfer Transaction

This example demonstrates a transaction to transfer $100 from Account A to Account B, with error handling and rollback.

  • : Uses to connect to a PostgreSQL database.
  • : starts a transaction with a specified isolation level ().
  • : runs SQL queries within the transaction.
  • : Checks for errors after each query and calls if any occur.
  • : Queries the balance to enforce a business rule (e.g., no negative balance).
  • : Calls to save changes or to undo them.
  • Install the PostgreSQL driver:
  • Ensure a PostgreSQL database with an table:

Common Issues and Mitigations

    • : Multiple transactions lock resources in conflicting orders.
    • : Access tables in a consistent order, use shorter transactions, and catch deadlock errors ( or database-specific errors) to retry.
    • : Long transactions hold locks, reducing concurrency.
    • : Optimize queries, use appropriate indexes, and break large transactions into smaller ones.
    • : Concurrent transactions overwrite each otherέΑβs changes.
    • : Use stricter isolation levels (e.g., ) or optimistic locking.
    • : Improper connection handling can lead to leaks.
    • : Always defer and ensure or is called.
  • : Pass a to and query methods to support cancellation and timeouts.
  • : Always call in error paths, even if fails, as some DBMSs require it.
  • : Leverage έΑβs built-in connection pooling by reusing the object.
  • : Choose the appropriate isolation level for your use case to balance consistency and performance.
  • : Test transaction failure scenarios (e.g., constraint violations, network issues) to ensure robust error handling.

Database transactions ensure data integrity by adhering to ACID properties. In Go, the package provides a clean and efficient way to manage transactions, with robust error handling and rollback support. By following best practices and understanding database state transitions, you can build reliable applications that maintain consistency even in the face of failures.

For further details, explore the Go documentation and your DBMSέΑβs transaction management features (e.g., PostgreSQLέΑβs MV assumptions.

items.title: Creating Custom File Systems: Implementing the FS Interface 7/9
items.author: Rez Moss
items.content:
Design Principles for Custom File Systems

When building a custom file system that implements Go's interface, you need to establish clear design principles that will guide your implementation decisions. These principles ensure your file system behaves predictably and integrates seamlessly with existing Go code.

Interface Compliance Requirements

The foundation of any custom file system is strict adherence to the interface contract. Your implementation must return consistent behavior that other Go packages can rely on. The core Open(name string) (fs.File, error) method serves as the entry point for all file operations, and its behavior sets the tone for your entire implementation.

Path handling requires careful attention. Your file system should accept forward slashes as path separators, regardless of the underlying platform. Invalid paths containing elements like ".." or absolute paths starting with "/" should return . This consistency allows your file system to work correctly with packages like or .

File names must be validated according to the interface specification. Empty names, names containing null bytes, or names with invalid Unicode sequences should trigger appropriate errors. Your implementation should also handle edge cases like "." for the current directory, which some callers might use to explore the file system root.

Error Handling Conventions

Error handling in file systems requires a delicate balance between providing useful information and maintaining security. The package defines several sentinel errors that your implementation should use consistently. should be returned when files or directories don't exist, while indicates access restrictions.

Custom error types become necessary when you need to provide additional context. However, ensure these errors still satisfy the expected interfaces. A well-designed file system often implements for operations that fail on specific paths, providing both the operation name and the path that caused the failure.

Consider the caller's perspective when crafting error messages. Avoid exposing internal implementation details that could confuse users or create security vulnerabilities. A HTTP-based file system, for instance, shouldn't leak server error details through file system error messages.

Thread Safety Considerations

Modern applications frequently access file systems concurrently, making thread safety a critical design consideration. Your file system implementation must handle multiple goroutines accessing the same resources without corruption or deadlocks.

The interface itself doesn't require specific thread safety guarantees, but practical usage patterns demand it. Multiple goroutines might call simultaneously, or different goroutines might read from files opened from the same file system instance. Your implementation should support these patterns safely.

Consider using read-write mutexes when your file system maintains internal state that supports both read and write operations. For read-heavy workloads, this allows multiple concurrent readers while still protecting against writers. However, be cautious about lock granularity - overly broad locks can create performance bottlenecks.

State management becomes particularly important in file systems that cache data or maintain connections to external resources. Design your caching strategies with concurrency in mind, potentially using sync.Map for concurrent access or implementing per-resource locking to reduce contention.

Memory-based file systems face unique challenges since they typically store all data in memory structures. These structures must support concurrent access while maintaining consistency. Consider using atomic operations for simple state changes and proper synchronization primitives for complex operations that span multiple data structures.

Building your first custom file system starts with understanding how to map abstract file operations to your underlying data storage. A memory-based implementation provides an excellent foundation for learning these concepts before moving to more complex storage backends.

Memory-Based File System Example

A memory-based file system stores all files and directories in RAM using Go's built-in data structures. This approach offers fast access and simplifies the initial implementation while teaching core concepts that apply to any storage backend.

The key insight here is separating the file system structure from the file handles returned to callers. The struct maintains the storage, while separate reader types handle individual file access. This separation allows multiple callers to open the same file simultaneously without interfering with each other.

File and Directory Simulation

Simulating files and directories requires implementing the various interfaces that callers expect. Files must implement , which includes methods for reading, seeking, and obtaining file information. Directories implement additional interfaces for listing contents.

Directory simulation requires additional consideration for how you'll represent directory entries. The interface provides a lightweight way to describe directory contents without requiring full file information upfront.

Path Validation and Security

Path validation forms a critical security boundary in your file system implementation. Beyond the basic check, you need to consider how paths map to your internal storage and prevent access to unintended resources.

Implement path normalization early in your request processing. Convert all paths to a canonical form that your internal logic can work with consistently. This typically means cleaning paths, resolving any relative components, and ensuring consistent separator usage.

Security considerations extend beyond path validation. Consider what information your error messages reveal. A file system that exposes whether a path doesn't exist versus lacking permission to access it might leak information about the underlying storage structure. Design your error responses to provide useful feedback without compromising security.

Consider implementing access controls at the file system level rather than relying solely on the underlying storage. This approach gives you fine-grained control over what callers can access and provides a consistent security model regardless of your storage backend.

Advanced Interface Implementations

Beyond the basic interface, Go provides several optional interfaces that can significantly improve your file system's performance and functionality. Implementing these interfaces allows your file system to integrate more deeply with the Go ecosystem and provide optimized operations for common use cases.

ReadDirFS for Directory Optimization

The interface provides a direct way to list directory contents without requiring callers to open a directory file first. This optimization becomes particularly valuable when your underlying storage can list directories more efficiently than the default file-based approach.

The performance benefit becomes clear when you consider network-based or database-backed file systems. Instead of opening a directory file and making multiple read calls, allows a single operation to retrieve all directory contents. This reduces both latency and resource usage.

Implementing also improves compatibility with Go's standard library functions. Functions like automatically detect and use this interface when available, providing better performance for directory traversal operations.

StatFS for Metadata Efficiency

The interface enables efficient metadata retrieval without opening files. This becomes crucial for operations that need file information but don't require file contents, such as directory listings that display file sizes or modification times.

For remote file systems, can dramatically reduce network round trips. Instead of opening a file to get its metadata, your implementation can query the remote system directly for file information, potentially batching multiple stat operations into a single request.

The interface also enables more sophisticated caching strategies. You can cache file metadata separately from file contents, allowing stat operations to return immediately from cache while still requiring network access for file reads.

GlobFS for Pattern Matching

The interface provides efficient pattern matching capabilities that many applications require. Rather than forcing callers to traverse entire directory trees manually, this interface allows your file system to optimize pattern matching based on its internal structure.

For database-backed file systems, enables pushing pattern matching down to the database level using SQL LIKE operations or similar constructs. This approach can significantly reduce the amount of data transferred and processed in your application.

Tree-structured storage systems can optimize glob operations by pruning search paths early. If a pattern doesn't match a directory prefix, the entire subtree can be skipped, dramatically reducing search time for complex directory hierarchies.

Advanced glob implementations might also support more sophisticated patterns than the basic shell-style matching. Regular expressions, SQL-style patterns, or custom matching logic can be implemented while still maintaining the standard interface contract.

The key to effective implementation lies in understanding your storage system's strengths. Memory-based systems can afford to check every path, while remote systems benefit from server-side filtering. Database systems might translate patterns into efficient queries, while cached systems could maintain pattern indexes for frequently used glob operations.

Robust error handling distinguishes professional file system implementations from simple prototypes. The package establishes specific error conventions that your implementation should follow to ensure consistent behavior across the Go ecosystem.

The type serves as the standard way to report path-specific errors in file system operations. This error type wraps the underlying error while providing context about the operation and path that failed.

Your file system should construct instances consistently across all operations. The field should use lowercase operation names that match the interface method being called. Common operations include "open", "stat", "readdir", and "glob".

The path in should be the original path provided by the caller, not your internal normalized version. This preserves the context that users need to understand and debug their code. However, be cautious about exposing sensitive information through error paths in security-sensitive applications.

Consistent Error Messages

Error message consistency helps users build reliable applications on top of your file system. The package defines several sentinel errors that have specific meanings, and your implementation should use these errors appropriately.

indicates that a requested file or directory doesn't exist. This error should be used consistently whether the path is completely invalid or points to a location that simply doesn't contain a file.

signals access control violations. Your file system should return this error when operations fail due to insufficient permissions, not when they fail for other reasons like network connectivity issues.

covers malformed inputs, including invalid paths, null bytes in filenames, or other parameter validation failures. This error helps distinguish between "you asked for something that doesn't exist" and "you asked for something incorrectly."

Timeout and Permission Errors

Network-based and remote file systems encounter additional error conditions that require careful handling. Timeout errors should provide enough context for callers to distinguish between different types of failures while maintaining security boundaries.

Implementing the and methods allows error-handling code to respond appropriately to different failure modes. Network clients can retry temporary errors but might handle permanent errors differently.

Permission errors require special consideration in multi-user or security-sensitive environments. Your error messages should provide enough information for legitimate debugging without exposing system internals that could aid attackers.

Consider implementing custom error types for domain-specific failures. A database-backed file system might define connection errors, while a cached file system could report cache coherency issues. These custom errors should still wrap appropriate standard errors when possible.

Error handling also extends to resource cleanup. Ensure that your file system properly handles partial failures and doesn't leak resources when operations are interrupted. This becomes particularly important for file systems that manage external connections, temporary files, or other system resources.

Testing Custom Implementations

Testing custom file systems requires a systematic approach that validates both interface compliance and implementation-specific behavior. The Go standard library provides tools to help verify your implementation meets the expected contracts, while additional testing strategies ensure robust behavior under various conditions.

Using testing/fstest.TestFS

The package provides , a comprehensive test suite that validates your file system against the standard interface requirements. This test suite checks hundreds of edge cases and interface compliance issues that are easy to miss in manual testing.

expects you to provide a list of paths that should exist in your test file system. The function then performs exhaustive testing of these paths and many invalid paths, ensuring your implementation handles edge cases correctly.

The test suite validates critical behaviors like path normalization, error handling, and interface compliance. It checks that your method returns appropriate errors for invalid paths, that directory listing works correctly, and that file reading behaves as expected.

Running early and frequently during development helps catch interface violations before they become deeply embedded in your implementation. The test suite is thorough but not exhaustive - it focuses on interface compliance rather than performance or implementation-specific features.

Beyond interface compliance, your file system needs unit tests that verify implementation-specific behavior, performance characteristics, and error conditions. These tests should focus on the unique aspects of your file system that doesn't cover.

Performance testing becomes crucial for file systems that will handle large numbers of files or frequent operations. Benchmark tests help identify performance regressions and validate optimization efforts.

Comprehensive testing requires systematically exploring edge cases that real applications might encounter. These tests often reveal subtle bugs that don't appear in normal usage patterns.

Resource exhaustion testing helps ensure your file system behaves gracefully under stress. These tests might create thousands of files, attempt to read extremely large files, or simulate network failures for remote file systems.

Integration testing with real Go packages validates that your file system works correctly in practical scenarios. Testing with , , or operations ensures compatibility with common use cases.

Implementing custom file systems becomes more meaningful when you understand how they solve practical problems. These real-world examples demonstrate different approaches to common challenges, each highlighting specific design decisions and implementation techniques.

An HTTP-based file system provides access to remote files through standard HTTP requests. This approach enables serving files from web servers, CDNs, or REST APIs while maintaining the familiar file system interface.

The HTTP file system demonstrates several key patterns. Caching reduces repeated network requests, while proper error mapping translates HTTP status codes into appropriate file system errors. The implementation handles network timeouts and connection failures gracefully.

For production use, consider implementing cache expiration based on HTTP cache headers, supporting authentication mechanisms, and handling partial content requests for large files. Range requests can significantly improve performance when clients only need portions of large files.

Cached File System Wrapper

A cached file system wrapper adds caching capabilities to any underlying file system. This pattern proves particularly useful for slow storage backends like network file systems or encrypted storage.

The cached file system wrapper demonstrates composition patterns where one file system enhances another. Cache eviction policies, TTL management, and memory usage tracking become critical concerns for production implementations.

Union File System Implementation

A union file system combines multiple underlying file systems, presenting them as a single unified interface. This pattern enables layered file systems, overlay mounts, and fallback mechanisms.

Union file systems require careful consideration of precedence rules, directory merging strategies, and conflict resolution. The implementation above uses a simple "first layer wins" approach, but production systems might need more sophisticated policies.

These real-world examples demonstrate that custom file systems solve diverse problems through different architectural approaches. HTTP file systems bridge network protocols, cached wrappers improve performance, and union systems provide flexible composition. Each pattern can be adapted and combined to meet specific application requirements while maintaining the standard Go file system interface.

items.title: controller-runtime/pkg/manager/signals
items.author: ΉιεΈψ╝Ήγ▒
items.content:
Implements Graceful Shutdown Pattern to safely handle signal-based program termination.

  • : Triggers graceful shutdown via context cancellation
  • : Forces immediate termination (user's strong intent)

Q1: What's the scope of the global variable and why prevent duplicate calls this way?

: Package-level global variable: Signal handlers must be . Multiple registrations make signal handling unpredictable. Using έΗΤ immediate panic implements fail-fast principle.

Q2: Why create and return a context instead of handling shutdown directly?

:

  • : Multiple goroutines can receive shutdown signal simultaneously
  • Standardized cancellation: Uses Go's standard cancellation mechanism
  • : Can be combined with other contexts

Q3: How does signal.Notify work internally with OS signals? What about multi-core or fork/spawn scenarios?

  • Go runtime registers signal handlers with the OS kernel
  • Uses platform-specific mechanisms: signalfd(Linux), kqueue(BSD/macOS), or signal(POSIX)
  • OS delivers signals to the process, Go runtime converts them to channel sends
  • : Signals are process-level, not thread-level - any thread can receive but Go runtime handles distribution
  • : Child processes inherit signal dispositions but need separate Go signal handlers
  1. signal.Notify(c, SIGTERM) έΗΤ Go runtime calls signal.signal(SIGTERM, handler)
  2. OS receives signal έΗΤ calls Go's C signal handler
  3. C handler έΗΤ Go runtime signal dispatcher έΗΤ channel send

Q4: What about goroutine lifecycle - doesn't this create a permanent goroutine that never gets cleaned up?

Goroutine Lifecycle Concern: Yes, this creates a that lives until process termination. Unlike closures that can be garbage collected, this goroutine:

  • Holds references to channel and function
  • Blocks on channel operations, preventing GC
  • - it's a system-level daemon
  • Gets cleaned up only when entire process exits

Q5: What happens to the signal handler goroutine when the main goroutine exits?

The signal handler goroutine dies with the entire process when the main goroutine terminates.

Even if signal.Notify() is registered, the signal handler may never run if the program exits first.

: The signal handler may receive signals and cancel context, but only if the main goroutine stays alive long enough. Go programs exit when main() returns, regardless of active goroutines.

Q6: Why handle two signals?

: First signal starts graceful shutdown, second signal forces immediate termination (common Ctrl+C twice pattern).

Allows buffering two signals that may arrive simultaneously or in quick succession. If buffer was 1 and two signals arrive before the goroutine processes them, the second signal could be lost.

: If only one signal is received, the goroutine will block forever on the second :

This is acceptable because the program terminates gracefully via context cancellation, making the blocking goroutine irrelevant.

  • In kubernetes sigs.k8s.io/controller-runtime

items.title: The Power of Idiomatic Go: What Makes It Different from Java and C#
items.author: jjpinto
items.content:
Idiomatic Go is more than a style έΑΦ it reflects the languageέΑβs simplicity, clarity, and philosophy, especially when contrasted with Java and C#.

  • Idiomatic Go = clear, consistent, and community-driven code
  • Java/C# = flexible, but less opinionated
  • GoέΑβs design and tooling make idioms essential, not optional
  • Embracing idioms = better teams, better code, better sleep ΏθαΕ

If you've spent any time in the Go community, you've probably heard the phrase "idiomatic Go" more times than you can count. But have you ever wondered why Go developers emphasize idioms more than those using Java or C#?

As someone whoέΑβs worked across multiple languages, IέΑβve noticed that Go has a unique culture around idioms έΑΦ and itέΑβs not just a stylistic preference. ItέΑβs deeply embedded in both the language's design and its community practices.

Ώθπι What Is "Idiomatic" Code in Go?

In everyday language, an idiom is a phrase like έΑεkick the bucketέΑζ έΑΦ you canέΑβt figure out its meaning just by looking at the individual words.

In programming, an idiom is a common and recognizable way of writing code that the community understands, expects, and uses. ItέΑβs not just about syntax έΑΦ itέΑβs about style, intent, and best practices.

In Go, writing idiomatic code means aligning with the languageέΑβs core values and conventions. You're not just writing code that works έΑΦ you're writing code that .

HereέΑβs what idiomatic Go code usually embodies:

  • έεΖ έΑΦ Easy for other Go developers to scan and understand
  • έεΖ έΑΦ Avoids unnecessary abstractions or clever tricks
  • έεΖ έΑΦ Leverages GoέΑβs strengths (like simplicity and concurrency)
  • έεΖ έΑΦ Follows community standards (naming, structure, error handling)

Idioms in Go act like a shared language έΑΦ they reduce friction, improve collaboration, and make Go projects feel familiar, even across teams.

Ώθπφ Why Idiomatic Go Matters More Than in Java or C#?

Go was built to be small and simple. It avoids features that often lead to complexity (like inheritance or method overloading). That means there are fewer ways to do things έΑΦ and more agreement on the έΑεrightέΑζ way.

2. Tooling That Keeps You in Line

GoέΑβs tooling is famously opinionated έΑΦ and thatέΑβs a good thing!

  • formats your code automatically. No arguments. No config.
  • ThereέΑβs no debate over tabs vs spaces έΑΦ Go just decides for you!
  • , , and other tools spot non-idiomatic patterns and enforce Go best practices.

The Go community values clarity over cleverness. Rob PikeέΑβs famous Go proverb says:

This mindset encourages patterns that are easy to read, easy to maintain, and easy to teach.

έγΨΎ╕Π How Java and C# Stack Up

Java and C# are powerful, mature languages έΑΦ but theyέΑβre also more flexible and layered. You can write object-oriented, functional, reactive, or procedural code depending on your team or framework.

That flexibility is great, but it also means:

  • ThereέΑβs no single έΑεidiomaticέΑζ way to do things
  • Style and structure vary widely between teams
  • Tooling is less opinionated έΑΦ you configure everything

In short: Java and C# have idioms, but theyέΑβre not as central or enforced as they are in Go.

ΏθΝΞ Real-World Benefits of Idiomatic Go

Because idiomatic Go leads to:

  • ΏθγΑ έΑΦ new devs can read and understand code quickly
  • ΏθΦΞ έΑΦ fewer debates about style or structure
  • Ώθπ╝ More maintainable codebases έΑΦ consistent patterns reduce bugs

ItέΑβs not just about aesthetics έΑΦ itέΑβs about developer velocity and team health.

LetέΑβs say you want to launch 100 goroutines and safely append to a shared slice.

You could do this in many ways έΑΦ ExecutorService, Parallel.For, synchronized, lock, ConcurrentBag, etc. But thereέΑβs no single idiomatic pattern that everyone agrees on.

Idiomatic Go isnέΑβt just a quirk έΑΦ itέΑβs a reflection of the languageέΑβs DNA. By keeping the language small and the tooling strict, Go encourages a shared way of writing code thatέΑβs easy to read, reason about, and maintain.

ThatέΑβs why idiomatic Go matters more έΑΦ and why itέΑβs worth learning and embracing if youέΑβre working in the language.

ΏθΤκ Ever wonder why beats ? trumps ? Or wins over 'packageNameLongerThanLife'?

Naming isnέΑβt just cosmeticέΑΦitέΑβs idiomatic.

Ώθπι It's not just what you nameέΑΦit's why you name it that way.

items.title: Database Schema Migration Cheatsheet with golang-migrate/migrate
items.author: Huseyn
items.content:
This cheatsheet provides a concise guide to using the library for database schema migrations. It covers installation, file structure, migration commands (up, down, force down), and best practices.

  • What is golang-migrate/migrate?

    • A CLI tool and Go library for managing database schema migrations.
    • Supports incremental, version-controlled, and reversible changes to database schemas.
    • Compatible with databases like PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite, and more.
    • Migrations are written as SQL files (or Go code for advanced cases).
    • : Applies changes to evolve the schema (e.g., create tables, add columns).
    • : Reverts changes to roll back the schema (e.g., drop tables, remove columns).
    • : Resolves issues with a "dirty" database by forcing a specific version.

Install the CLI tool for your operating system.

  • Go installed (for Go-based CLI compilation, optional).
  • A supported database (e.g., PostgreSQL, MySQL) with connection details.

Install via Package Managers

  curl  https://github.com/golang-migrate/migrate/releases/download/v4.17.1/migrate.linux-amd64.tar.gz | xvz

  • : Files are named <timestamp>_<description>.<up/down>.sql.

    • Example: 202507120001_create_users_table.down.sql for dropping it.

Run migrations using the CLI with a database connection string and migration path.

  • : Shows detailed output.

    • : 
  migrate  database/migrations/  down
  • Reverts the last applied migration.

  • Use to revert the last migrations (e.g., ).

    • Force Down (Fix Dirty Database): 
  migrate  database/migrations/  force VERSION
  • Use when a migration fails and marks the database as "dirty," preventing further migrations.
  • Replace with the desired migration version (e.g., ).
  • : Ensure the database state matches the forced version to avoid inconsistencies.

  • Example: If migration fails, fix the error in the file, then run:

items.title: When io.ReadAll Becomes a Performance Bottleneck in Go
items.author: Eke Enyinnaya Diala
items.content:
When working with HTTP responses, file I/O, or data streams in Go, developers often reach for as the go-to solution for reading data into memory. It's convenient, it's in the standard library, most API documentations use it in examples, and it "just works." For most applications processing small, predictable data sizes, this is perfectly fine.

However, when dealing with large or unknown-sized data streamsέΑΦsuch as file uploads, API responses with variable payload sizes, or data processing pipelinesέΑΦthe performance characteristics of can become problematic.

In this article, we'll explore different approaches to reading data in Go and examine when the convenience of comes at a significant performance cost.

The Setup: Two Approaches to Reading Data

Let's examine two common patterns for reading data from an :

Approach 1: The "Obvious" Way with io.ReadAll

Simple, clean, and straightforward. This is what most developers would naturally reach for. All API documentations I have seen use this in their example.

Approach 2: The Buffered Approach

This approach uses with . It's slightly more verbose but, as we'll see, there's a good reason to prefer it.

The buffered approach also allows us to optimize things further using , which allows us to pass a preallocated buffer of our desired size (which can be from a ) and as such eliminate the cost of continuously creating new slices only to discard them after copying the data. As always there are no free lunches and as such we must benchmark this to be certain there are practical benefits:

This optimization is particularly valuable in high-throughput scenarios where the same operation is performed repeatedly.

The Benchmark: Putting Theory to the Test

I created a comprehensive benchmark to test both approaches across different data sizes:

The Results: A Performance Story

Here are the benchmark results on an Apple M1 Pro:

BenchmarkReadMethods/IoReadAll_1024-10         3337831    355.7 ns/op    2864 B/op    4 allocs/op
BenchmarkReadMethods/Buffered_1024-10          6806582    176.2 ns/op    1120 B/op    3 allocs/op
BenchmarkReadMethods/IoReadAll_10000-10         265746   4556 ns/op    46128 B/op   11 allocs/op
BenchmarkReadMethods/Buffered_10000-10         1216825    983.6 ns/op   10336 B/op    3 allocs/op
BenchmarkReadMethods/IoReadAll_100000-10        27645  44683 ns/op   514363 B/op   19 allocs/op
BenchmarkReadMethods/Buffered_100000-10        144283   8272 ns/op   106594 B/op    3 allocs/op
BenchmarkReadMethods/IoReadAll_1000000-10        2845 414515 ns/op  5241270 B/op   32 allocs/op
BenchmarkReadMethods/Buffered_1000000-10        15804  76015 ns/op  1007733 B/op    3 allocs/op

Breaking Down the Numbers

Performance Gap Widens with Data Size

  • : Buffered approach is ~2x faster
  • : Buffered approach is ~4.6x faster
  • : Buffered approach is ~5.4x faster
  • : Buffered approach is ~5.5x faster

Memory Usage: Scaling Becomes Problematic

The memory usage patterns reveal where struggles:

  • : uses 5.2MB of memory, while the buffered approach uses only ~1MB
  • : The buffered approach consistently uses memory proportional to the input size
  • For typical API responses (1-10KB): Both approaches perform similarly with minimal overhead
  • : Performance differences become significant with data >100KB

Allocations Tell the Story

  • : Allocation count grows with data size (32 allocations for 1MB)
  • : Consistently only 3 allocations regardless of data size

Why io.ReadAll Performs So Poorly

The performance difference isn't accidentalέΑΦit's architectural. Here's what's happening under the hood:

The io.ReadAll Growth Strategy

(Based on Go 1.24.2 source code: /usr/local/go/src/io/io.go)

When doesn't know the final size of the data (which is almost always the case), it follows this pattern:

The growth cycle works like this:

  1. : 512 bytes capacity, 0 length
  2. : Into the available capacity ()
  3. : Update length to include new data ()
  4. : When , no more space available
  5. : triggers Go's slice growth (typically doubling)
  6. : removes the extra byte, keeping only actual data
  7. : Continue reading into the new capacity
  • : Each reallocation creates temporary copies of all existing data
  • : Multiple buffer reallocations as data grows beyond each capacity threshold
  • : Repeated copying operations with each growth phase

The bytes.Buffer Growth Strategy

The buffered approach using with is fundamentally different:

bytes.Buffer Growth Algorithm:(Based on Go 1.24.2 source code: /usr/local/go/src/bytes/buffer.go)

  • Starts with a nil slice (no initial allocation)
  • For initial small allocations (έΚν64 bytes), allocates exactly what's needed with 64-byte capacity ()
  • Has optimizations to avoid unnecessary allocations:
    • Can grow by reslicing existing capacity when possible ()
    • Can slide data down instead of reallocating when there's enough capacity
  • When new allocation is needed, uses which typically doubles capacity ()
  • The growth pattern is more conservative and optimized than naive doubling

(Based on Go standard library implementation)

  • uses a 32KB buffer (const copyBuffer = 32 * 1024)
  • Data is read and written in 32KB chunks
  • This chunked approach means grows in predictable increments
  • The buffer doesn't need to guess the final sizeέΑΦit grows incrementally as data arrives
  • Each growth decision is made with concrete information about actual data size
  • Minimizes unnecessary reallocations
  • Avoids the repeated copy penalty of unknown-size scenarios
  • Maintains consistent allocation patterns
  • Benefits from Go's optimized slice growth algorithms

Further Optimization with io.CopyBuffer

How io.CopyBuffer + sync.Pool Works:

  • internally allocates a 32KB buffer for each operation
  • lets you provide a buffer (of your desired size), avoiding this allocation
  • provides efficient buffer reuse across goroutines
  • The combination eliminates temporary allocations entirely for the copy operation
  • Only the final growth allocations remain

This optimization is particularly valuable in high-throughput scenarios where the same operation is performed repeatedly, such as web servers processing many requests.

This isn't just academicέΑΦthese performance characteristics have real implications:

We do not know the size of the packet the server is sending to us and we do not want to run out of memory given that we know io.ReadAll is not memory efficient for large datasets.

When processing files of unknown size, the buffered approach can save significant memory and CPU cycles. ensures we can have significantly less system calls as it reads the data in 32kb chunks.

Memory-Constrained Environments

In containerized environments or systems with limited memory, the memory overhead of for large payloads could contribute to memory pressure, especially under high load with many concurrent operations processing large data streams.

The 32KB Buffer Sweet Spot

The function's 32KB buffer size isn't arbitraryέΑΦit's optimized for:

  • : Reduces the number of read/write system calls
  • : 32KB fits comfortably in CPU caches
  • : Aligns well with typical network packet sizes
  • : Works efficiently with most storage block sizes

However, this also means:

  • : Even tiny reads will allocate at least 32KB temporarily
  • : grows in response to actual data, not speculation
  • : Growth patterns are more deterministic than 's guessing game

When to Use Each Approach

Use io.ReadAll when (most common cases):

  • Processing typical API responses (<50KB)
  • Working with small, bounded data sizes
  • Prototyping or development where convenience matters
  • Applications where the performance difference doesn't impact user experience
  • : If your data is consistently under 100KB, is usually fine

Use the buffered approach when:

  • Processing file uploads or downloads
  • Handling data streams of unknown or potentially large size (>100KB)
  • Building systems that need to handle variable payload sizes efficiently
  • Memory efficiency is critical for your use case
  • Processing many large payloads concurrently

Use the io.CopyBuffer optimization when:

  • Building high-throughput systems processing large data repeatedly
  • Every allocation matters for your performance profile (rare)
  • You've profiled your application and identified this as a bottleneck
  • Processing thousands of large payloads per second
  1. : is fine for most use cases, but problematic for large or variable-sized data
  2. : Performance differences become significant around 100KB+ data sizes
  3. : The buffered approach scales memory usage linearly, while can use 2-5x more memory
  4. : For typical web APIs and small files, the convenience of usually outweighs the performance cost
  5. : Use the buffered approach when processing large or unknown-sized data streams
  6. Profile before optimizing: Measure whether this optimization actually impacts your application's performance
  7. Further optimization exists: with provides additional benefits for high-throughput scenarios

remains a perfectly reasonable choice for most Go applications processing typical data sizes. However, when building systems that handle large files, variable-sized payloads, or operate under memory constraints, understanding these performance characteristics becomes important.

The key is recognizing when you've moved from the "small, predictable data" use case to the "large or unknown-sized data" scenario. For file uploads, data processing pipelines, or APIs that might return large responses, the buffered approach provides better memory efficiency and more predictable performance.

As with most performance optimizations, measure first, optimize second. If you're processing mostly small data, stick with for its simplicity. If you're dealing with large or variable data sizes, consider the buffered approach as a better foundation for scalable systems.

The complete benchmark code and examples from this article are available in the accompanying repository.

items.title: Golang: Transform any Cobra CLI into an MCP server
items.author: Nick Powell
items.content:
Ophis converts Cobra command trees into Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers. This means Claude Desktop (and other MCP clients) can call your CLI commands as structured tools with proper type checking and parameter validation. The user implements a command factory interface to create fresh command instances for each call, preventing state pollution while maintaining thread safety.

I have had success turning my own CLIs and other open source CLIs into mcp servers. Here are images showing Claude interacting with my Kubernetes cluster using helm and kubectl.

This feels like where CLI tooling is headed - structured integration with AI rather than raw shell access. Would love feedback from the community.

items.title: Car Batteries: The Magic Core of Electronics
items.author: ersajay
items.content:
Title: "Car Batteries: The Magic Core of Electronics"

A Chat with Hagrid in the Garage The garage smelled of motor oil and magicέΑΦHagridέΑβs giant hands were wrapped around a car battery, his bushy beard twitching. έΑεThis hereέΑβs no ordinary battery,έΑζ he rumbled, tapping it with a wrench that sparked faintly. έΑεItέΑβs the heart of your carέΑβs magicέΑΦlike a phoenix feather core, but for engines.έΑζ

I leaned in. έΑεJust a battery, right?έΑζ

έΑεJust a battery?έΑζ Hagrid boomed. έΑεWhy, this little fella powers everything from your headlights to the enchanted maps in your dashboard! Mess it up, and youέΑβll be stuck faster than a first-year on a broomstick.έΑζ

LetέΑβs unlock its secrets.

  • The BatteryέΑβs Magic: Core of the CarέΑβs Spell A car battery is the PhilosopherέΑβs Stone of your vehicleέΑΦa lead-acid (or AGM/lithium) cauldron that stores electrical energy. When you turn the key, it unleashes a surge of power, like casting Alohomora to start the engine. Key Charms (Specs):

Voltage: 12.6V at full charge (peak magic), 13.5-14.7V when the engineέΑβs running (like a wand channeling magic). Capacity: Measured in Ah (Amp-hours)έΑΦthink of it as how many spells it can cast before needing a recharge.

Lead-Acid: The old school wand (3-5 years life, $70-$150). AGM: The holly and phoenix feather (4-7 years, $180-$280, better for cold climates).

Lithium Hybrid: The elder wand (8-10 years, $250+έΑΦPrius owners swear by it).

Danger Zone: Letting voltage drop below 11.8V? ThatέΑβs like breaking your wandέΑΦirreversible sulfation (a curse that kills batteries).

  • Chargers: The Healing Spells for Batteries Even the strongest cores need healing. Enter smart chargersέΑΦthe Murtlap Essence of car batteries.

Smart Charger: Like Episkey for drained batteries. Uses pulse technology (Pat. US2024157832) to extend life by 30% (MIT 2025). Perfect for winter storage. Jump Starter: The Portus charm for emergencies. Jumps engines at -30┬░C (NOCO Genius 10 works like a charm). But donέΑβt leave it connected >3 minutesέΑΦyouέΑβll fry the ECU (like over-casting Expelliarmus).

Solar Charger: The Sunlight Charm for RVs. But beware cloudy daysέΑΦoutput drops to <0.5A (useless as a broken telescope).

Critical Ritual: Charge with the hood open! Hydrogen gas (from the battery) explodes at 4% concentrationέΑΦlike mixing Felix Felicis with firewhiskey.

  • Buying a Battery: Diagon Alley vs. Knockturn Alley Not all batteries are created equal. HagridέΑβs rule: έΑεStick to the trusted shops, or youέΑβll end up with a dud like a fake wand from Borgin and Burkes.έΑζ

WalmartέΑβs EverStart Plus ($70): The WeasleyέΑβs Wizard Wheezes specialέΑΦsame cells as CostcoέΑβs $100 battery, but cheaper. EverStart Maxx ($180): Carbon-infused plates (like dragon heartstring cores) for -30┬░C starts. Worth the Galleons.

RED FLAG: Avoid shelved batteries >6 monthsέΑΦthey lose 12% capacity (electrolyte stratification, the DementorέΑβs kiss of batteries).

Perk: $15 core refund (lead recyclingέΑΦlike returning a borrowed wand to OllivanderέΑβs).

  • Replacing a Battery: The Sacred Ritual Changing a battery is like mending a broken wandέΑΦmess up the steps, and youέΑβll regret it. The Proper Sequence:

Prep: Wait 10 mins post-ignition (ECU hibernationέΑΦdonέΑβt wake it too soon, or itέΑβll forget spells). Disconnect: Negative FIRST (black cable), then Positive (red). Reverse order = 1,000┬░C firework show (like Fiendfyre under the hood).

Install: Torque terminals to 4-6Nm (overtightening cracks postsέΑΦlike snapping a wand core).

Activate: Slow-charge 12hrs (awakens the magicέΑΦadds 22% lifespan).

Dealer: $280 (battery) + $120 (labor) = Gringotts-level prices. Walmart: $210 (battery) + FREE install = Hogsmeade bargain.

DIY: $180 (battery) + 0 labor = Muggle savings.

  • How Long Do They Last? The Curse of Time A batteryέΑβs life depends on three things: type, temp, and care.

Lead-Acid: 3-5 years rated, but Florida taxis die in 22 months (heat boils electrolyteέΑΦlike leaving a potion on the fire too long). AGM: 4-7 years rated, Canadian SUVs hit 7.5 years (cold slows decayέΑΦlike a Freezing Charm).

Lithium Hybrid: 8-10 years rated, Prius record 11 years (but BMS failure is the HorcruxέΑΦhard to destroy, but deadly when it fails).

Weekly Highway Runs: 20 mins burns sulfate crystals (like Scourgify for the battery). Winter Blanket: Insulation boosts cranking amps by 40% (like Warming Charm for your core).

Summer Shield: Reflective hood cover drops engine bay temp by 20┬░C (keeps the cauldron cool).

  • Safety: Avoiding Battery Curses Batteries are powerful magicέΑΦtreat them with respect.

Never Wear Metal Watches: 2,400 cases/year of wrist arc burns (like touching a Cursed Fire doorknob). Ignition Off During Swap: BMW ECUs brick easily ($2,000 repairέΑΦlike losing your wand in the Forbidden Forest).

Acid-proof gloves + goggles (electrolyte pH=0.8έΑΦstronger than Polyjuice Potion). Vaseline on terminals (70% corrosion reductionέΑΦlike Reparo for metal).

The Final Charm Car batteries are the unsung heroes of the muggle and magic worldsέΑΦquietly powering everything from headlights to enchanted dashboards.

έΑεRemember,έΑζ Hagrid said, patting the battery gently, έΑεTreat it right, and itέΑβll never leave you stranded. Mess up, and youέΑβll be hoofinέΑβ it like a first-year who missed the Hogwarts Express.έΑζ

And I realizedέΑΦsome magic is best kept simple.

Written by a witch who once fried a battery trying to charge it with a wand. (Spoiler: It sparked. A lot.) ΏθΦΜ A batteryέΑβs magic is in the care you give it.

items.title: A Practical Guide to GoέΑβs Fan-in and Fan-out Concurrency Patterns
items.author: Jones Charles
items.content:
A Practical Guide to GoέΑβs Fan-in and Fan-out Concurrency Patterns

1. Welcome to Go Concurrency

Hey there, Go devs! If youέΑβve been coding in Go for a year or two, youέΑβve probably played with and έΑΦthe bread and butter of GoέΑβs concurrency model. ItέΑβs lightweight, elegant, and feels like it was made for todayέΑβs multicore world. Among the many concurrency tricks up GoέΑβs sleeve, and are two patterns that stand out. TheyέΑβre like the peanut butter and jelly of parallel programming: one gathers data, the other spreads it out.

This post is for devs whoέΑβve got the basics down and want to level up with real-world concurrency skills. Whether youέΑβre aggregating logs from a dozen microservices or parallelizing image processing, these patterns can make your code faster, cleaner, and more fun to write. IέΑβve been coding Go for a decadeέΑΦsince it was a quirky underdogέΑΦand IέΑβve leaned on Fan-in and Fan-out in projects like distributed crawlers and batch processors. IέΑβll share code, examples, and some scars from the trenches to help you master them.

Why care? Imagine collecting logs from multiple nodes into one stream (Fan-in) or splitting a pile of tasks across workers (Fan-out). These patterns arenέΑβt just academicέΑΦthey solve everyday problems with GoέΑβs concurrency magic. LetέΑβs dive in, starting with the basics!

2. Fan-in and Fan-out: The Basics

Before we get hands-on, letέΑβs define these patterns and see why theyέΑβre so handy. Built on and , theyέΑβre simple but pack a punch.

2.1 Fan-in: The Data Gatherer

Fan-in is all about merging multiple input channels into one output channel. Think of it as streams flowing into a river. Got logs from five servers? Fan-in pulls them into a single pipeline for processing.

  • : It simplifies downstream logic by giving you one channel to rule them all. Cleaner code, less headache.
  • : Log aggregation, sensor data collectionέΑΦanytime youέΑβre wrangling multiple data sources.
Input 1 ----\
Input 2 ----+----> Output
Input 3 ----/
2.2 Fan-out: The Task Splitter

Fan-out does the opposite: splits one input channel into multiple output channels. ItέΑβs like a river branching into tributaries, each handled by a worker . Need to process 100 images? Fan-out hands them to parallel workers.

  • : It taps into multicore CPUs, slashing processing time with parallel power.
  • : Batch jobs, distributed computingέΑΦthink image compression or API calls.
Input ----> Output 1
      ----> Output 2
      ----> Output 3
2.3 Why Go Makes This Easy

GoέΑβs concurrency primitives make these patterns a breeze:

  • : Lightweight threadsέΑΦspawn thousands without sweating memory.
  • : Lock-free data pipes, perfect for safe communication.
  • : A ninja move for juggling multiple channels.

Fan-in gathers, Fan-out distributes. Together? TheyέΑβre a powerhouse. LetέΑβs see them in action.

3. Fan-in: Merging Made Simple

Fan-in is your go-to when dataέΑβs coming from all directions and you need it in one place. LetέΑβs break it down with code and a real-world spin.

Fan-in takes multiple input channels, assigns each a , and funnels their data into one output channel. A often ensures everything wraps up neatly when the inputs dry up.

: Imagine couriers collecting mail from different towns and dropping it at one post office.

HereέΑβs a quick Fan-in exampleέΑΦmerging strings from multiple workers:

  • merges three channels into one.
  • Each worker pumps data into .
  • closes when all inputs finish.

Run it, and youέΑβll see a merged stream like:

Worker 1: 0
Worker 2: 0
Worker 3: 0
...

OrderέΑβs not guaranteedέΑΦconcurrencyέΑβs wild like that!

3.3 Real Talk: Log Aggregation

I once built a log collector for a microservices setup. Each service sent logs via a channel, and Fan-in merged them into one stream for storage. Adding a new service? Just toss in another channel. It was scalable and stupidly simple.

  • : Forget to close an input channel, and its hangs forever. Always in producers.
  • : Need order? Add timestamps and sort laterέΑΦFan-in wonέΑβt do it for you.

Fan-inέΑβs a lifesaver for data wrangling, but letέΑβs switch gears to Fan-out.

4. Fan-out: Parallel Power Unleashed

Fan-outέΑβs your ticket to parallelismέΑΦspreading tasks across workers to crush bottlenecks. LetέΑβs dig in.

Fan-out takes one input channel and feeds it to multiple output channels, each tied to a . ItέΑβs divide-and-conquer with a Go twist.

: A chef passing ingredients to sous-chefs for simultaneous prep.

HereέΑβs Fan-out doubling some numbers across workers:

  • splits the input across three workers.
  • Each worker doubles its share and sends it out.
Worker 0: 2
Worker 1: 4
Worker 2: 6
...
4.3 Real Talk: Image Processing

In an image compression app, Fan-out distributed uploads to workers. One worker per image, running in parallelέΑΦprocessing time dropped from minutes to seconds. Users loved it.

  • : Workers might not split tasks evenly. Buffering or smarter allocation can help.
  • : Slow workers can clog the input. Add a buffer (e.g., ).

Fan-outέΑβs a beast for parallel tasksέΑΦnext, weέΑβll combine it with Fan-in!

5. Fan-in + Fan-out: The Ultimate Team-Up

Fan-in and Fan-out are awesome solo, but together? TheyέΑβre a concurrency dream team. Split tasks, then gather resultsέΑΦperfect for complex workflows. LetέΑβs see how they play together.

Real-world problems often need both: to distribute work across workers, and to collect the results. ItέΑβs like chopping veggies in parallel (Fan-out) then plating the meal (Fan-in). This combo crushes performance bottlenecks while keeping your code tidy.

LetέΑβs combine them to square some numbersέΑΦFan-out spreads the work, Fan-in merges the squares:

  • splits numbers across three workers to compute squares.
  • gathers the results into one channel.
  • ties it togetherέΑΦdistribute, process, collect.
5.3 Real Talk: Web Crawler

I used this combo in a web crawler. Fan-out sent URLs to workers fetching pages in parallel, then Fan-in merged the HTML for analysis. Hundreds of pages processed in minutesέΑΦtry that with a single thread!

URL Queue --> Fan-out --> Workers --> Fan-in --> Analysis
  • : Match it to CPU cores () for CPU-bound tasks, or scale up for I/O stuff like network calls.
  • : Use to kill goroutines cleanly if something goes wrong.
  • : Log worker activity to spot imbalancesέΑΦtrust me, youέΑβll thank yourself later.

Combining them is where the magic happens. LetέΑβs talk best practices next.

6. Best Practices and Lessons from the Wild

After a decade of Go, IέΑβve learned Fan-in and Fan-out arenέΑβt just έΑεset it and forget it.έΑζ HereέΑβs how to make them shineέΑΦand avoid facepalms.

  • : Thousands ning sound cool, but memory and scheduling suffer. Cap workers based on your hardwareέΑΦstart with CPU cores as a baseline.
  • : Unbuffered channels can stall if workers lag. A small buffer (e.g., ) keeps things flowing, but donέΑβt overstuff it.
  • : Unclosed channels = deadlocks. Use everywhere, and pair Fan-in with .
  • : Add an error channel for workers to report issues. Centralize chaos:
  • : I once lost hours to a log system hanging because an input channel never closed. Fix: Add timeouts with and use to debug.
  • : In an image processor, fixed workers choked at peak load. Solution: Dynamically tweak worker count based on queue size.
  • : Syncs goroutines like a champ.
  • golang.org/x/sync/errgroup: Error handling + context supportέΑΦgold for complex flows.
  • : Profiles goroutine leaks or bottlenecks. Add import _ "net/http/pprof" and poke around.

These tricks keep your concurrency smooth and your sanity intact.

7. Wrapping Up: Your Concurrency Journey

Fan-in and Fan-out are GoέΑβs concurrency superheroes. Fan-in tames chaotic data streams; Fan-out unleashes parallel power. Together, they tackle big problems with elegant code. IέΑβve loved watching Go grow from a έΑεtoyέΑζ to a server-side titan, and these patterns have been my trusty sidekicks in log systems, crawlers, and more.

HereέΑβs my challenge: . Parallelize a file processor with Fan-out, merge logs with Fan-in, or build a mini-pipeline. Break stuff, fix it, learn. Hit a snag? Drop a commentέΑΦIέΑβm all ears for swapping tips.

Looking forward, these patterns are gold in a world of microservices and cloud scale. GoέΑβs concurrency edge keeps it relevant, and tools like hint at more goodies to come. For me, the joyέΑβs in the dance of and έΑΦitέΑβs Go at its best.

  1. Start smallέΑΦone pattern at a time.
  2. Mix them for real-world wins.
  3. Steal ideas from the Go community.

Thanks for joining me on this concurrency ride! WhatέΑβs your next Go experiment? LetέΑβs chat below!
