items.title: LDR: The Little Prince of Light, Tracing Shadows & Tuning Stars
items.author: ersajay
items.content:
A Chat with the Fox in the Dunes The desert stretched golden, its sands glowing like honey under the sun. I was following the foxέΑβs paw prints when I spotted itέΑΦa small, unassuming disc, no bigger than a ladybug, half-buried in the dust.

έΑεThatέΑβs an LDR,έΑζ the fox said, tail flicking. έΑεA Light Dependent Resistor. A little prince of light, you might say.έΑζ

I knelt. έΑεIt looks ordinary,έΑζ I said.

έΑεOrdinary things often guard the most secrets,έΑζ the fox replied. έΑεAsk the rose. Ask the stars. Ask this LDR.έΑζ

Let me tell you its story.

  • What Is an LDR? (The Shadow-Keeper of Light) An LDR, or photoresistor, is no ordinary component. ItέΑβs a cadmium sulfide (CdS) semiconductorέΑΦa tiny detective that measures light by changing its electrical resistance. Think of it as a sand gauge for photons: when light hits, electrons leap free (conducting current), and in the dark, they settle (raising resistance). Key Traits (Like a RoseέΑβs Thorns):

Photon Impact: Light energizes electrons, freeing them to conduct (like a sunbeam waking a desert flower). Dark State: Electrons settle, raising resistance to 1-10 M╬σ (like a cactus closing its blooms at night).

CdS (detects visible light, 400-700nmέΑΦrose-pink). CdSe (infrared-shiftedέΑΦmoon-silver).

PbS (IR detectionέΑΦstar-dust).

Scientific Whisper: Conductivity follows ╧Δ=e(╬╝nn+╬╝pp) \sigma = e(\mu_n n + \mu_p p) ╧Δ=e(╬╝nέΑΜn+╬╝pέΑΜp). Dark currents (10nA) surge to 100╬╝A under lightέΑΦlike a desert spring after rain.

  • LDR vs. Other Light Sensors: A Tale of Three Guardians Light sensors are like desert travelersέΑΦeach with their own strengths.

LDR: The cactus of sensors. Cheap ($0.03 in bulk), slow (10-100ms response), but tough (withstands 100V surges). Best for dusk lights, toys, and low-cost magic (like tending a garden of solar lanterns). Photodiode: The greenhouse orchid. Expensive ($1.50-$25), fast (1ns-1╬╝s), but fragile (ESD-sensitive). Rules fiber optics and medical tools (like polishing a diamond lens).

Phototransistor: The desert fox. Efficient ($0.50-$5), quicker (2-5╬╝s), but fussy (non-linear). Dominates industrial detection (like spotting a hawk in the sky).

Industry Verdict: LDRs are the everyday heroέΑΦsimple, reliable, and everywhere.

  • Why Engineers (and Foxes) Love LDRs Engineers donέΑβt just use LDRsέΑΦthey trust them. HereέΑβs why:

ΏθΤ░ Economic Magic: Mass-produced at $0.03/unit (cheaper than sand in the desert). 10x cheaper than photodiodes for light detection. έγκ Simple as a Sunset: Works with a voltage dividerέΑΦno op-amps needed. Just Vout=Vin├ΩRfixedRfixed+RLDR V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{in}} \times \frac{R_{\text{fixed}}}{R_{\text{fixed}} + R_{\text{LDR}}} VoutέΑΜ=VinέΑΜ├ΩRfixedέΑΜ+RLDRέΑΜRfixedέΑΜέΑΜ. Like tuning a harp with one hand.

ΏθδκΎ╕Π Tough as a Baobab: Withstands 100V surges (UL 1449 certified). CdS self-healsέΑΦno drama, no tears (unlike a broken telescope).

ΏθΝκΎ╕Π Steady as the Stars: Resistance drifts <0.5%/┬░C (vs. 2%/┬░C for photodiodes). No calibration, no fuss (like the foxέΑβs daily ritual).

ΏθΦπ Forever Young: Lifetime >100,000 hours (11+ years). Maintenance-freeέΑΦjust set it and forget it (like B612έΑβs volcanoes).

έΑεImportant things are invisible to the eye,έΑζ the fox said. έΑεThis little one? ItέΑβs a giant.έΑζ

  • Global Adventures: LDRs Taming Light, From Cities to Stars LDRs donέΑβt just live in labsέΑΦtheyέΑβre explorers, like the Little Prince himself.

Smart Cities: TokyoέΑβs 10M+ LDRs dim streetlights at dawn, saving 2.3TWh/year (like tucking stars back into the sky). Automotive Magic: TeslaέΑβs LDR arrays adjust dashboard brightness (0-10,000 nits), even predicting tunnel lights (Pat. US2023156789A1έΑΦlike a sunflower turning to the sun).

Precision Farms: John Deere tractors use LDRs to detect chlorophyll decay, auto-adjusting irrigation (like the fox teaching the wheat to thrive).

Medical Miracles: FDA-cleared LDRs measure UVB for psoriasis phototherapy (like a healerέΑβs gentle touch).

  • Circuit Design: Building with LDRs (Like Fixing B612έΑβs Volcano) Working with LDRs is like repairing a broken asteroidέΑΦsimple, but precise. Industrial Light Meter (NORPS-12 LDR):

Parts: LDR, 10k╬σ resistor, ADS1115 ADC. Logic: lux=10(logέΒκ10(RLDR)έΙΤbm) \text{lux} = 10^{\left( \frac{\log_{10}(R_{\text{LDR}}) - b}{m} \right)} lux=10(mlog10έΑΜ(RLDRέΑΜ)έΙΤbέΑΜ). If lux > 20000, alert: έΑεSolar flare detected!έΑζ (like warning the rose of a sandstorm).

The Future: LDRs & the Stars of Tomorrow WhatέΑβs next for our little prince of light?

Quantum LDRs (Tokyo U): CdSe quantum dots boost IR sensitivity to 1500nm (like giving the fox night visionέΑΦNature Photonics 2026). Self-Powered LDRs (MIT): Piezoelectric films generate 3╬╝W from raindrops (like turning dew into starlight).

The Secret of the Shadow-Keeper LDRs arenέΑβt flashy. They donέΑβt glow like LEDs or roar like motors. TheyέΑβre the reason your streetlights dim at dawn, your TeslaέΑβs screen doesnέΑβt blind you, and a farmerέΑβs tractor knows when to water the wheat.

έΑεWhat makes you special?έΑζ I asked, as I brushed sand off the LDR.

It didnέΑβt answer. It just sat there, quiet as the desert, as the stars, as time itself.

And I realizedέΑΦsome keepers donέΑβt need to be big. They just need to watch.

Written by a wanderer who once built a solar lantern with an LDR. (Spoiler: It worked. And lit up the foxέΑβs path.) ΏθΝ╡ You become responsible, forever, for the light you once overlooked.

items.title: Parameter Passing in Golang: The Ultimate Truth
items.author: mahdi
items.content:
We often learn that Go parameters are either passed by value or by reference. For example, basic types (ints, bools, etc.), arrays and structs are έΑεby value,έΑζ while things like pointers, slices, maps, and channels are often called έΑεby reference.έΑζ In practice this terminology can be misleading. In Go, every function parameter is passed by value (i.e. copied). That copy might be a simple value (for an int or an array) or a more complex descriptor (for a slice, string, or map).

  • Passed by value: numbers, booleans, arrays, structs, etc. (the entire value is copied)

  • Reference-like types: pointers, strings, slices, maps, channels (the pointer or descriptor is copied)

LetέΑβs walk through some examples to see what really happens.

Passing an Array (By Value)

func do(b [3]int) int {
    b[0] = 0
    return b[1]
}

func main() {
    a := [3]int{1, 2, 3}
    v := do(a)
    fmt.Println(a, v)  // [1 2 3] 2
}

In this example, do takes a fixed-size array b [3]int. When we call do(a), Go makes a copy of a and passes it to do. Inside do, setting b[0] = 0 only affects the copied array, not the original. This is why after calling do, the original a is still [1 2 3]. As the Go blog explains, έΑεwhen you assign or pass around an array value you will make a copy of its contents.έΑζ (If you want to modify the callerέΑβs array, youέΑβd have to pass a pointer to the array, e.g. * [3]int.)

Passing a Map (Pointer-Like Behavior) 

func do(m1 map[int]int) {
    m1[3] = 1
    m1[4] = 3
}

func main() {
    m := map[int]int{4: 2}
    fmt.Println(m)  // map[4:2]
    do(m)
    fmt.Println(m)  // map[3:1 4:3]
}

Here, do takes a map. Internally, a Go map is implemented as a pointer to a runtime hash table structure. When we call do(m), Go copies the map descriptor (which is effectively a pointer) and passes it to do. The code m1[3] = 1 then mutates the underlying hash table. Because the descriptor copy still points to the same map data, the original map m in main sees the changes. In other words, the argument m and the parameter m1 both refer to the same map in memory. As Alex Edwards notes, Go έΑεimplements a map as a pointer to a runtime.hmap struct,έΑζ so passing a map to a function gives you a copy of that pointer έΑΥ modifying the map will mutate the original data.

The Slice Puzzle: Append and Capacity 

func do(b []int) int {
    b = append(b, 4)
    b[0] = 0
    return b[1]
}

func main() {
    a1 := []int{1, 2, 3}
    fmt.Printf("len %d cap %d\n", len(a1), cap(a1))  // len 3 cap 3
    v1 := do(a1)
    fmt.Println(a1, v1)  // [1 2 3] 2
}

Many people expect slices to behave like maps in the previous example (with changes showing up outside). But here, after calling do(a1), the slice a1 is still [1 2 3]. What happened? Remember that a slice value in Go is itself a 3-word descriptor: it contains a pointer to an underlying array, a length, and a capacity. In our example, a1 := []int{1,2,3} has length 3 and capacity 3. Inside do, we do b = append(b, 4). Since the capacity of b was only 3, append allocates a new underlying array (with larger capacity), copies the old elements over, and returns a new slice header with that new array pointer. Then b[0] = 0 writes to this new array. Meanwhile, the original slice a1 in main still points to the old array (which was untouched). In short, the append caused a reallocation, so b inside do ended up pointing to a different array than a1 does. The assignment b[0] = 0 did not affect a1έΑβs data. As Alex Edwards explains, the slice returned by append έΑεmay or may not point to the same underlying arrayέΑζ depending on capacity. If it allocates a new array, the functionέΑβs copy of the slice header is changed without affecting the callerέΑβs slice. This is why a1 remains [1 2 3] even though do returned 0 at index 1 of its (new) slice. All Parameters Are Passed By Value

The key insight is: Go always copies the function arguments. The phrase έΑεpass-by-valueέΑζ means every argument is copied. There is no hidden έΑεpass-by-referenceέΑζ semantics in Go. Even when we passed a pointer, a slice, or a map, the pointer or descriptor was copied by value. As Alex Edwards emphasizes, έΑεparameters are always a copy of the arguments. That is, they are always passed by value.έΑζ.

In other words, a function parameter is never an alias for the callerέΑβs variable. The only way a function can change the callerέΑβs variable is if the caller passes in something like a pointer and the function dereferences it to write through to the original data.

Descriptors for Strings, Slices, and Maps

In GoέΑβs runtime, complex types like strings, slices, and maps are built on small header (descriptor) values that contain pointers under the hood. For example, a Go string is a two-word struct: a pointer to the UTF-8 data and a length. A slice is a three-word struct: a pointer to the first element of an array, the slice length, and the slice capacity. A map is not described by just a few words in Go code, but internally itέΑβs a pointer to a runtime hash table. Thus, when you pass a map or channel to a function, you are copying that pointer-like header. Any element writes (like m1[3]=...) update the shared data. Notice that in each case, what gets copied is the header or pointer, not the entire contents. So when we say slices or maps are έΑεreference types,έΑζ we mean their headers contain pointers to shared data. But the headers themselves are passed by value (copied) into functions.

Passing a Pointer to a Slice 

func do(b *[]int) int {
    *b = append(*b, 4)
    (*b)[0] = 0
    return (*b)[1]
}

func main() {
    a1 := []int{1, 2, 3}
    fmt.Printf("len %d cap %d\n", len(a1), cap(a1))  // len 3 cap 3
    v1 := do(&a1)
    fmt.Println(a1, v1)  // [0 2 3 4] 2
}

What if we really want the function to be able to grow the callerέΑβs slice? Here do takes a pointer to a slice (*[]int). Now, inside do we do *b = append(*b, 4) έΑΥ this dereferences the pointer and reassigns the callerέΑβs slice variable to the new slice value returned by append. Then (*b)[0] = 0 writes to the shared array. When we run this, the output shows a1 has become [0 2 3 4]. By passing &a1, we gave do the address of the slice header. Any assignment to *b actually updates a1 in main. As the official blog notes, writing *s = append(*s, έΑο) will be έΑεwritten-through to the memory address of the scores argumentέΑζ. In other words, using a pointer-to-slice parameter forces append and other reassignments to affect the original slice variable.

  • Go always passes function arguments by value. Even a pointer, slice, map or channel parameter is still a copy of whatever header or pointer you passed in. There is no hidden pass-by-reference mechanism.

  • Basic types, arrays, structs: Passing them copies the entire value. Mutating the parameter never changes the callerέΑβs variable unless you passed a pointer to it.

  • Slices, maps, channels: Each has an internal header (pointer + length/capacity or pointer to a hash table). Passing one of these copies the header. Mutations to the underlying data (e.g. setting a slice element or a map entry) affect the original data structure. However, reassigning the slice or map (like b = append(b,έΑο) or m = make(mapέΑο)) only changes the local copy of the header, not the callerέΑβs copy.

  • Strings: Immutable, but a string header (pointer + length) is also passed by value. Slicing a string creates a new header pointing into the same data.

In short, donέΑβt think in terms of έΑεGo passes this by reference.έΑζ Think of it as έΑεGo copies values,έΑζ and the effect depends on what those values are. If the value contains a pointer to shared data (as in slices or maps), then functions can mutate that shared data. If you need a function to update a variable itself (like extend a slice or change a struct), you must pass a pointer to it. This understanding will help avoid confusing surprises and make your Go code behavior predictable.

items.title: MongoDB Change Streams and Go
items.author: MongoDB Guests
items.content:
This tutorial was written by Ado Kukic.

Change streams allow you to subscribe to real-time updates in your MongoDB collections and databases. With the MongoDB Go Driver, you can tap into these streams and build reactive applications that respond to data changes in MongoDB instantly. You can build features like real-time notifications, collaborative apps, or kick off different workflows based on changes to your data.

In this tutorial, weέΑβll take a look at how you can work with MongoDB change streams when building Go applications. WeέΑβll use the native MongoDB Go Driver and MongoDB Atlas to showcase various use cases that rely on change streams.

For this application, IέΑβll be using:

Existing knowledge of MongoDB and the Go programming language is required.

Setting up MongoDB change streams in Go

The first thing weέΑβll do is set up a basic Go application that connects to our MongoDB Atlas cluster and sets up a global database change stream that will fire off an event any time a change is made in our database. The code will look like the following:

This is our basic change stream implementation that is going to update us any time any data is changed in the stockTrader database. For now, it will just print the full document to the terminal. After you start up the application, simply go into your MongoDB Atlas Data Explorer or MongoDB Compass and add some data. In the console, youέΑβll see an output like this whenever you add or update data:

Alternatively, I could scope down the change stream to only a particular collection with:

In this case, I would only be watching for changes on the trades collection, not the entire stockTrader database. I could take it a step further and watch my entire cluster and all databases with:

I would advise against watching an entire cluster, though. There are use cases where it may be done, but typically, youέΑβd want your change streams to be focused and optimized for specific use cases.

Your database and even an individual collection is going to be generating a lot of data. Especially a stock trading application, like the hypothetical one weέΑβre building in this tutorial, could generate thousands of records every second. Subscribing to all of these events would generate too much noise to be of any practical use. This is where filtering can greatly help. We can set up filtering on our change streams to only alert us when certain criteria are met.

We can use MongoDBέΑβs powerful aggregation pipelines to filter out irrelevant data and only surface data we really care to be alerted about. To see an example of that, letέΑβs update our existing code so that the change stream only fires off when a trade is conducted on a particular stock, the trade is a sell, and the quantity of stock in that trade is greater than 50,000.

LetέΑβs update our code to the following:

We have now updated our change stream to trigger only on specific conditions. Specifically, this change stream will trigger only if all of the following criteria are met:

  • The stock quantity of the trade is greater than 50,000.
  • The stock is AAPL, MSFT, or GOOG.
  • The action of the trade is a sell.

You may use this as an indicator that the major blue-chip tech stocks are selling off, for example.

If we were to now add this document in our database:

If you want to try it yourself, here is the document:

{
    "_id":{"$oid":"68029db0c9034b66740d7406"}, 
    "action": "sell",
    "stock": "MSFT",
    "quantity": 75000
}

WeέΑβd see the change stream fire off, and we could act on this data:

We can do all sorts of filtering to really fine-tune when the change stream fires off an alert. Here are a couple of additional examples:

Handling change stream interruptions

Change streams work great as long as the application is running, but what happens when the application crashes? On restarting the app, change streams resume listening from the current moment, meaning any changes that happened during the downtime may be lost. But if you want to build fault tolerance and resilience and get the change stream notifications for any items that should have been processed via the change stream while your app was not running, MongoDB has a concept of a resume token that keeps track of change streams that have already been processed. LetέΑβs implement the resume token functionality into our app. I will also simplify our change stream aggregation to only look at the quantity of shares in a trade.

With resume token functionality implemented, now we donέΑβt have to worry about missing change stream events if our application crashes. A good way to test this is to start the application to make sure itέΑβs working. Insert a document into your collection:

{
  "_id":{"$oid":"6814eb8c3f593c417814d5ce"},
  "quantity": 100000
}

You should see that event captured by the change stream and displayed in the terminal.

Then, close your application, and while the application is not running, add a few more documents to your collection that should trigger the change stream. Start the application up again, and youέΑβll see all of the items you added once the app is running again.

The way this works is every time a change stream operation executes, weέΑβll update the collection with our . When the app is restarted, weέΑβll start our change stream from that token and process any events that we may have missed while the application was not running, continuously updating the and data with new events.

I chose to store the resume token information in my MongoDB database, but you can also store this token as a file in your application, a different database, or wherever you see fit.

Additionally, if we look at our console now and insert a document that triggers a change stream, weέΑβll see our resume token loaded as well as updated after an event:

Working with large documents and change streams

When working with large datasets, you may run into an issue where the size of the change stream event exceeds 16MiB. As of MongoDB 6.0.9, we have a $changeStreamSplitLargeEvent aggregation that can help you split the large events and process events that exceed 16MiB.

Although you are unlikely to run into this, I want to show you a complete example of how you can handle this with the MongoDB Go Driver. To do this, I will update the existing application to do a few things to create this scenario of working with data that exceeds 16MiB. First, weέΑβll create a script that continuously adds data to the database. WeέΑβll add one small document, and then one large document that is almost 16MiB in size. Finally, weέΑβll perform an update operation on that large file. Next, weέΑβll update our collection to enable it to retrieve a document both pre and post update, so that when we do the update, weέΑβll get back two documents that together exceed the 16MiB limit. Finally, weέΑβll implement our $changeStreamSplitLargeEvent logic to split apart the larger events and still process them. The code looks like this:

I know this is a lot of new code, but IέΑβve tried my best to comment on the important elements to give you a clear understanding of what is going on. I encourage you to copy this code and try it yourself to get a better feel for how itέΑβs working. The way this app works now:

  • Every five seconds, a new document will be inserted into the collection.
  • The document inserted will be either a small document or a large document that is a little over 8MiB in size.
  • Next, weέΑβll do an update operation on the large document. This will return both a pre and post image of the document, which will exceed the 16MiB threshold.
  • But, due to our $changeStreamSplitLargeEvent aggregation and accompanying functions for processing it, weέΑβll handle it just fine.

The output should look something like this:

2025/04/18 14:00:11  NEW CHANGE EVENT DETECTED 
2025/04/18 14:00:11 CHANGE EVENT: Received non-split event
2025/04/18 14:00:11 CHANGE EVENT: insert operation detected
2025/04/18 14:00:11 CHANGE EVENT: Document ID: ObjectID
2025/04/18 14:00:11 CHANGE EVENT: Fields count: 9
2025/04/18 14:00:11 CHANGE EVENT: Stock: AAPL
2025/04/18 14:00:11 CHANGE EVENT: Quantity: 100000
2025/04/18 14:00:11 CHANGE EVENT: Action: buy
2025/04/18 14:00:11 Saving resume token: : 
2025/04/18 14:00:11  CHANGE EVENT PROCESSING COMPLETE 
2025/04/18 14:00:11  NEW CHANGE EVENT DETECTED 
2025/04/18 14:00:11 Current resume token: :

2025/04/18 14:00:11 CHANGE EVENT: Stock: MSFT
2025/04/18 14:00:11 CHANGE EVENT: Quantity: 60000
2025/04/18 14:00:11 CHANGE EVENT: Action: sell
2025/04/18 14:00:11 Saving resume token: : 
2025/04/18 14:00:11  CHANGE EVENT PROCESSING COMPLETE

2025/04/18 14:00:17 Successfully updated document with large field
2025/04/18 14:00:18  NEW CHANGE EVENT DETECTED 
2025/04/18 14:00:18 Current resume token: : 
2025/04/18 14:00:18 CHANGE EVENT: Received fragment 1 of 2
2025/04/18 14:00:18 PROCESSING FRAGMENT: 1 of 2
2025/04/18 14:00:18 FRAGMENT CONTAINS FIELDS: fullDocument _id splitEvent documentKey ns wallTime clusterTime operationType updateDescription]
2025/04/18 14:00:18 FRAGMENT HAS fullDocument with 10 fields
2025/04/18 14:00:18 FRAGMENT DATA - Stock: AAPL
2025/04/18 14:00:18 FRAGMENT DATA - Quantity: 100000
2025/04/18 14:00:18 FRAGMENT HAS updateDescription with 3 fields
2025/04/18 14:00:18 Saving resume token: : 
2025/04/18 14:00:18  CHANGE EVENT PROCESSING COMPLETE 
2025/04/18 14:00:19  NEW CHANGE EVENT DETECTED 
2025/04/18 14:00:19 Current resume token: : 
2025/04/18 14:00:19 CHANGE EVENT: Received fragment 2 of 2
2025/04/18 14:00:19 CHANGE EVENT: Received all 2 fragments this event
2025/04/18 14:00:19 PROCESSING FRAGMENT: 2 of 2
2025/04/18 14:00:19 FRAGMENT CONTAINS FIELDS: _id splitEvent fullDocumentBeforeChange]
2025/04/18 14:00:19 FRAGMENT HAS fullDocumentBeforeChange with 10 fields
2025/04/18 14:00:19 Saving resume token: : 
2025/04/18 14:00:19  CHANGE EVENT PROCESSING COMPLETE

Now, your change stream implementation is optimized for your specific use case, fault-tolerant with resume tokens, and optimized for working with large datasets.

Best practices and performance considerations

Change streams unlock a ton of potential and use cases for your MongoDB data. To wrap up this tutorial, letέΑβs cover a couple of best practices and good things to know about change streams and how to implement them for the best outcome.

: Keep your aggregation pipelines as lean as possible. Complex pipelines might impact performance.

: Always capture and persist the resume token after processing events to ensure you can resume accurately.

: Implement robust error handling, including reconnect logic if the change stream is invalidated.

: Close change streams appropriately to avoid resource leaks.

In this tutorial, we learned how to work with MongoDB change streams using the MongoDB Go Driver to build an application that allowed us to tap into our MongoDB data as it was added and take action. But this is just the beginning. With change streams, you can do so much more. One awesome use case is using change streams to build fully collaborative, real-time applications. Or in the case of our fictional Stock Trader application, we could expand it to detect fraudulent trades or great buying opportunities in real time.

Learn more about MongoDB change streams and join our community to share how you are using them in your applications. I hope you enjoyed this tutorial, and happy hacking!

items.title: Anti-pattern: Overwriting return value inside defer
items.author: Harutyun Mardirossian
items.content:
Over the years, I have observed numerous examples of code overriding the return value within a defer statement. This pattern is quite common in my professional experience. In almost every project I have been involved in, I have encountered this exact scenario at some point.

Many believe that the function will return errors.New(έΑεsomething went wrongέΑζ), as the is considered the "root cause" of the error. However, the actual output of this function is errors.New(έΑεsomething else went wrongέΑζ) and here's why.

The function returns a named parameter () that is overwritten within the statement. This approach is intended to stop the programέΑβs execution if the statement fails. While this may appear to be a clever solution, it can result in unexpected behavior and introduce bugs into the code.

The first time I encountered this issue was while debugging a pool of workers that were randomly causing invalid memory address or nil pointer dereference. My team and I were relatively new to Go. It was my first year after switching from PHP, and it took almost a week to identify the root cause of the problem.

A few days ago, I came across a similar concept in my ongoing project. I posted a question on LinkedIn to understand how many people are aware of this anti-pattern. This pattern can lead to unexpected behavior and bugs in the code.

In the function signature , the variable is a result parameter. According to the Go specification:

  • It is declared and initialized to its zero value (nil for error types) upon entry to the function.
  • It is treated like any other local variable within the function body. You can read from it and assign to it.
  • Crucially, if the function executes a return statement without arguments (a "naked" return), the current value of the result parameter is what's returned.

When function returns err, it's explicitly returning the current value of this specific variable. This variable err lives in the function's stack frame and acts as the designated memory location for the return value.

Let's trace the execution of step-by-step.

The run function is called and the result parameter is declared and initialized within function's stack frame. Its initial value is and 

+------------------------------------+
| run() Stack Frame                  |
+------------------------------------+
| err (Result Parameter): nil        | <-- the return slot
+------------------------------------+

The statement compiles into a closure. The closure captures a reference to the variable. This closure is then pushed onto a per-goroutine stack of deferred calls.

The expression err = doSomething() is executed. The error value is assigned to the result parameter . The value of in the stack frame is now errors.New("something went wrong").

+------------------------------------+
| run() Stack Frame                  |
+------------------------------------+
| err (Result Parameter): "err_A"    | <-- "something went wrong"
+------------------------------------+

In this step things are getting complicated. The statement triggers a two-phase process:

  • The return expression is evaluated and locks in its current value as the value to be used for the return. errors.New("something went wrong") for now.

  • Before the function officially returns control to , the runtime executes any deferred calls in LIFO order. Inside the closure, the statement is run. Because the closure holds a reference to the result parameter err, this assignment overwrites the value in 's stack frame. The value of the result parameter err is now errors.New("something else went wrong").

+------------------------------------+
| run() Stack Frame                  |
|                                    |
|   +----------------------------+   |
|   | defer() closure executing  |   |
|   |                            |   |
|   | err = doSomethingElse()    | --+-\
|   +----------------------------+   | |
|                                    | |
| err (Result Parameter): "err_A"    | | <-- overwrites err
+------------------------------------+ |
                                       |
<--------------------------------------+

When completes the value of the result parameter err is overwritten.

+------------------------------------+
| run() Stack Frame                  |
+------------------------------------+
| err (Result Parameter): "err_B"    | <-- "something else went wrong"
+------------------------------------+

The function now completes its return sequence, returning the current value from the err slot. The value returned is or "something else went wrong".

At this point, you might ask a question: "But I don't see anything wrong here." Perhaps, if you have noticed the danger, my congratulations. LetέΑβs have a beer together.

LetέΑβs take a look at the scenario I faced five years ago and explore the reasons behind the week-long debugging process during my junior year.

A pool of parallel workers processing a huge file containing a list of JSON parameters. The idea was simple: read -> unmarshal into struct -> notify -> return the result in a channel.

The function was responsible for sending a message to Kafka. The function itself was executed by the worker.

We were not provided with the exact content of the JSON file we were reading. Instead, we were given a small chunk, which were used to construct our result struct. The JSON keys may contain invalid values. For example, instead of an object, there might be a number. This will result in the unmarshaling failing, and an error will be returned.

This return statement will initiate the deferred closureέΑβs execution. The will be executed, overriding the error returned by the function with . The return value of the function when fails will be . The worker will then push a value to the channel SomeOtherType{Result: nil}.

In the runtime, when accessing the field within the result of , the program will cause a panic.

The defer block completely broke the error path of the program. The required object was not initialized but was still returned. This error required a significant amount of time and effort to identify and rectify. Even experienced developers overlooked this error. I was able to reproduce this error by accidentally passing an empty JSON to be parsed.

Honestly, I have no idea what the workers initially wrote in such a way. I also don't know the intention behind this design decision. The project was handed over in the ready-to-production state. Even unit tests were written in a way that guaranteed to fail, and this case was never tested.

Unnamed return value scenario

Let's also discuss the case when a function returns a local variable as an error. Here, the f.run() function will return errors.New(έΑεsomething went wrongέΑζ).

In this scenario, when is called and A stack frame is created. It contains a slot for the local variable and a separate, anonymous slot for the return value.

+------------------------------------+
| run() Stack Frame                  |
+------------------------------------+
| __return_value_0 (hidden): nil     | <-- return slot
| err (local variable):     nil      | <-- local variable
+------------------------------------+

After executing the local err variable is updated.

+------------------------------------+
| run() Stack Frame                  |
+------------------------------------+
| __return_value_0 (hidden): nil     |
| err (local variable):     "err_A"  | <-- "something went wrong"
+------------------------------------+

At this point return is triggeted. This is the most important difference. The copy the value of the local err variable into the hidden return slot." This action happens before the defer is executed.

+------------------------------------+
| run() Stack Frame                  |
+------------------------------------+
| __return_value_0 (hidden): "err_A" | <-- RETURN VALUE IS NOW LOCKED IN
| err (local variable):      "err_A" |
+------------------------------------+

The deferred closure runs. It has a reference to the local variable, not the hidden return slot.

+------------------------------------+
| run() Stack Frame                  |
+------------------------------------+
| __return_value_0 (hidden): "err_A" | <-- Unchanged
| err (local variable):      "err_B" | <-- Overwritten
+------------------------------------+

The function returns the value from its hidden return slot. The value returned is "err_A".

The return mechanism in Go is more complex because defer and named return values create a multi-step process that gives the programmer explicit control over the function's exit logic. This explicit two-phase process is a distinguishing feature of Go. In other languages, the process is more opaque. When a return is hit in a try block, the language runtime knows a finally block must run, but the interaction with the return value itself is often less defined and considered an anti-pattern to exploit.

items.title: How I Cut My Local Development Setup Time by 95% Using Makefile
items.author: Renu Bhati
items.content:
How one simple file transformed my development workflow from 10 minutes to 30 seconds

As a developer, I was constantly typing the same Docker and database commands over and over. Starting PostgreSQL containers, running migrations, creating databases - it was getting repetitive and error-prone.

Setting up my local development environment was taking forever every time I switched between projects or started fresh. I needed something to make local development setup quick and consistent.

That's when I discovered I could use Makefiles to automate all these database operations for faster local development. Here's what I learned.

A Makefile is a simple text file that contains commands you can run with the command. While most people think it's only for compiling C programs, it's actually great for automating any repetitive tasks - including database operations.

My Database Makefile for Local Development

Here's the Makefile I created to speed up my local development setup with PostgreSQL:

MAKEFILE_LIST |  | 
    docker run  postgres  5432:5432 root secret  postgres


    docker stop postgres
    docker postgres


    docker  postgres createdb root root simple_bank


    docker  postgres dropdb simple_bank


    migrate  db/migration  up


    migrate  db/migration  down

This starts a PostgreSQL container with:

    This stops and removes the PostgreSQL container completely.

    This creates a new database called inside the running PostgreSQL container.

    This deletes the database.

    This runs all pending database migrations from the folder.

    This rolls back the last migration.

    Why Use Makefile for Local Development?

    Before Makefile (slow setup):

    
docker run  postgres  5432:5432 root secret  postgres
docker  postgres createdb root root simple_bank
migrate  db/migration  up

    After Makefile (quick setup):

    
make postgres
make createdb
make migrateup

    Now I can get my local development environment running in seconds instead of minutes!

    One really useful feature I added is a help command that shows all available commands:

    Available commands:
  createdb         Create database
  dropdb           Delete database
  help             Show all available commands
  migratedown      Rollback database migrations
  migrateup        Run database migrations
  postgres         Start PostgreSQL container
  remove-postgres  Stop and remove PostgreSQL container

    You can also just run without any command, and it will show the help automatically.

    The help command works by:

    1. Adding after each command
    2. Using a special help target that parses these descriptions

    3. Setting so alone shows help

    4. Create a file named in your project root

    5. Copy the commands above and modify them for your project:

      • Change database name from to your database name
      • Update username/password if needed
      • Adjust the migration path if different

    6. Run commands using

    • The indentation in Makefile must be tabs, not spaces
    • The line tells Make that these aren't file names
    • Make sure Docker is running before using these commands
    • Install tool if you want to use migration commands

    Common Local Development Workflow

    Here's how I typically use these commands to set up my local development environment:

    
make postgres
make createdb
make migrateup


make remove-postgres
make postgres
make createdb
make migrateup


make remove-postgres

    This workflow gets me from zero to coding in under 30 seconds!

    Benefits for Local Development

    1. - From zero to coding in 30 seconds
    2. - I don't have to type long Docker commands
    3. - Same setup works on any machine
    4. - Quick tear down and setup
    5. - New developers can start immediately

    Using Makefiles for local development database operations has been a game-changer for my workflow. Instead of memorizing complex Docker and migration commands, I just run simple commands and get my development environment ready instantly.

    If you're tired of slow local development setup and want to start coding faster, give this approach a try. Start with basic commands like the ones I shared, and you'll quickly see how much time it saves in your daily development routine.

    make postgres      
make remove-postgres 
make createdb      
make dropdb        
make migrateup     
make migratedown

items.title: ΏθΣ╖ΏθΠ╗έΑΞέβΓΎ╕Π Golang CI/CD Done Right: Build a Rock-Solid Pipeline with GitHub Actions
items.author: Giovanni De Giorgio
items.content:
• Did you know I just came back from a vacation? ΏθΝ┤
  • Built a simple Go CLI with Cobra
  • Automated PR checks, linting, and testing with GitHub Actions
  • Enforced Conventional Commits and semantic versioning
  • Generated changelogs and cross-platform releases with Goreleaser
  • Secured the pipeline using CodeQL and Dependabot

I just got back from a much-needed vacation, and during that time, I finally sat down and read The Phoenix Project. If youέΑβre not familiar with it, itέΑβs a novel about IT, DevOps, and the chaos that can happen when delivery processes are broken.

ItέΑβs part entertaining, part terrifying, and completely inspiring.

So when I returned to my Golang CLI projects, I decided to revisit and improve my CI/CD setup from the ground up. In this post, I will walk you through the stack I ended up with: a clean, modular, and production-ready CI/CD pipeline built entirely with GitHub Actions.

ΏθΤκ What I Learned from

The story really drove home how much trouble teams can get into without:

  • Automated security checks to catch vulnerabilities early
  • Reliable, automated testing to prevent regressions
  • Controlled deployments that minimize risk
  • Formal change management to track and govern what gets released

Without these safeguards, teams face outages, firefighting, and lost time, problems that could have been avoided with better automation and process.

This reinforced for me that CI/CD is more than just running builds and tests. ItέΑβs about embedding control, safety, and visibility into every step of software delivery.

Ώθοτ Create a Simple Golang Application

IέΑβve always been fascinated by how easy and powerful it is to build CLI applications in Go. With the help of Cobra, creating a structured, user-friendly command-line tool takes just a few lines of code.

go run main.go

Hello, world!

ΏθΥζ It All Starts with a Pull Request

The first step in building a reliable CI/CD pipeline is to ensure consistency and quality at the source your pull requests. If every PR follows a standard format and passes basic checks, the rest of the pipeline becomes much more predictable and automated.

To enable automated versioning and changelog generation later in the pipeline, I decided to adopt Conventional Commits. These are structured commit messages that follow a simple pattern:

feat: add new controller endpoint
fix: resolve panic on nil pointer in handler
chore: update dependencies

The next step is to . Manual checks donέΑβt scale.

  • έεΖ Ensure PR Title follows Conventional Commits
  • έεΖ Ensure all code builds successfully
  • έεΖ Run tests automatically
  • έεΖ Enforce formatting and linting rules

.github/workflows/pr-checks.yml

The initial results are promising, confirming our basic CI checks work as expected. Now, letέΑβs push forward and expand the pipelineέΑβs capabilities.

In The Phoenix Project, Bill Palmer, the VP of IT Operations, faces major headaches because developers are inconsistently managing versioning and leading to confusion, deployment failures, and firefighting. This real-world scenario highlights how crucial it is to properly version every code change.

With structured commit messages in place, we can now introduce and automate directly within our CI/CD pipeline.

By analyzing commit history, we can:

  • Determine the appropriate version bump (major, minor, or patch)
  • Automatically generate a well-formatted
  • Create and push a GitHub tag for the release

This not only enforces consistent versioning across releases, but also removes the manual overhead of maintaining changelogs and tagging code, a critical step toward reliable, reproducible deployments.

Once the project is ready for a new release, we can trigger an on-demand GitHub Actions workflow to automatically generate a new tag based on commit history.

.github/workflows/create-new-tag.yml

We can now respond to new tag events by automatically generating and publishing a changelog.

.github/workflows/changelog.yml

As our CLI evolves, itέΑβs important to demonstrate how feature development ties directly into our CI/CD workflow. LetέΑβs implement a simple enhancement: a flag that allows users to personalize the greeting message.

For example, running the following command:

go run main.go --name Giovanni

Once our pull request has been reviewed, approved, and successfully merged, ensuring all checks have passed, we can trigger the release workflow.

With everything in place, our pipeline now effortlessly handles releases, tagging, changelog updates and letting us focus on what truly matters: writing great code.

ΏθΥο Stop running

WeέΑβre almost there but thereέΑβs one crucial piece missing.

We donέΑβt want to clone the entire project every time I want to run ; I need to execute the binary directly. And hereέΑβs the twist: I have a Raspberry Pi with an ARM processor, a Mac with Apple Silicon, and a tiny Linux box so I want my binary to run seamlessly everywhere!

To solve this challenge, IέΑβll be using a powerful tool that automates building and publishing binaries for multiple platforms. With a single configuration YAML file, Goreleaser simplifies cross-platform releases, ensuring our CLI runs smoothly on ARM, Apple Silicon, Linux, and more all without manual hassle.

.github/workflows/package-hello-cli.yml

ThatέΑβs really incredible, donέΑβt you think? Ώθνψ

ΏθΦΡ Securing Your Application: CodeQL and Dependabot

No CI/CD pipeline is complete without a strong focus on and . As your application grows, so does the attack surface and the complexity of keeping everything up to date. Fortunately, GitHub provides two powerful tools to help us stay ahead: and .

Ώθπι CodeQL: Automated Security Analysis

is GitHubέΑβs semantic code analysis engine. It allows you to query your codebase like a database, identifying security vulnerabilities and logic flaws before they make it to production.

Once integrated into your workflow, CodeQL will:

  • Automatically scan your code
  • Detect common vulnerability patterns like SQL injection, command injection, or unsafe deserialization

By running CodeQL scans regularly, you introduce a proactive layer of defense directly into your development cycle, catching bugs early and enforcing security hygiene with minimal effort.

.github/workflows/codeql.yml

ΏθΦΕ Dependabot: Automated Dependency Updates

Modern applications rely on open-source libraries and keeping those up to date is essential. helps you do exactly that by automatically scanning your and other dependency files, opening pull requests when:

  • A newer version is available
  • A known vulnerability has been reported in a dependency

έεΖ Wrap-Up: A Pipeline You Can Trust

In this journey, we started with a simple idea :

building a lightweight CLI tool in Go and used it as a foundation to construct a solid, secure, and automated CI/CD workflow using GitHub Actions.

By layering in tools and best practices like:

  • for linting, testing, and compiling
  • Semantic commit enforcement to drive meaningful versioning
  • Automated changelog generation and
  • Multi-platform binary publishing with Goreleaser
  • with CodeQL
  • via Dependabot

έΑοweέΑβve established a pipeline that not only supports continuous delivery, but also builds trust through automation, clarity, and repeatability.

Whether youέΑβre building a toy CLI or the next critical internal tool, think of this as a good foundation you can tweak and grow depending on what your project needs.

  • ΏθΥα έΑΥ Gene Kim, Kevin Behr, George Spafford

    A novel that inspired this postέΑβs focus on flow, feedback, and continual improvement in software delivery.

  • Automate, customize, and execute software workflows directly in your GitHub repo.

  • GitHubέΑβs static analysis engine for discovering vulnerabilities in your code.

  • Keeps your dependencies up-to-date and secure with automated pull requests.

  • Build and release Go binaries for multiple platforms with ease.

  • A standardized format for writing commit messages that power semantic versioning.

items.title: The 510 Thread Battery: HogwartsέΑβ Universal Power Charm for Vapes & Beyond
items.author: ersajay
items.content:
A Chat with Hermione in Diagon Alley The cobblestones of Diagon Alley hummed with magicέΑΦowls fluttered, cauldrons bubbled, and a sign creaked above a shop: έΑεWeasleyέΑβs Wizarding Batteries.έΑζ Inside, Hermione Granger was squinting at a tiny silver cylinder, her brow furrowed like sheέΑβd just solved a Riddikulus puzzle.

έΑεItέΑβs a 510 thread battery,έΑζ she said, holding it up. έΑεNot magic, but close. A universal power charm for vape techέΑΦfits nearly any cartridge, like Alohomora for electronics.έΑζ

I leaned in. έΑεBut itέΑβs just a battery, right?έΑζ

έΑεJust a battery?έΑζ Hermione scoffed. έΑεThis is the PhilosopherέΑβs Stone of power unitsέΑΦcompatible, reliable, and hiding secrets even Snape would envy.έΑζ

LetέΑβs unmask its magic.

  • What Is a 510 Thread Battery? (The Universal Power Charm) The 510 thread battery is no ordinary power unit. ItέΑβs a wizardέΑβs toolέΑΦengineered with precise threading (10mm diameter, 0.5mm pitch) to snap onto nearly any vape cartridge, like a wand fitting a wand holster. Unlike proprietary systems (locked like Gringotts vaults), its universal design lets you mix brandsέΑΦno more έΑεthis cartridge only fits that batteryέΑζ nonsense. Key Spells (Specs):

Voltage Range: 3.2V-4.2V (adjustable in advanced models, like Diffindo with a twist). Capacity: 150mAh-650mAh (200-400 puffs per chargeέΑΦenough to last a Quidditch match).

Temperature Tolerance: -20┬░C to 50┬░C (survives car gloveboxes in Arizona wintersέΑΦtougher than a Blast-Ended Skrewt).

Safety Charms: Short-circuit protection, over-discharge cutoff (UL 8139-certifiedέΑΦlike Protego for circuits).

Health Reality: A 2024 Johns Hopkins study found vaping aerosols hold 2,000+ chemicalsέΑΦ6x more than we thought. έΑεMagic has costs,έΑζ Hermione muttered. έΑεEven this one.έΑζ

  • Why 510 Rules the Vape World (Like Dumbledore Rules Hogwarts) Proprietary pods are like Hogwarts housesέΑΦloyal, but limited. 510 batteries? TheyέΑβre the Great HallέΑΦopen to all.

Compatibility: 95% cartridge compatibility (ISO 8140:2015 threadingέΑΦlike a universal translation charm). Proprietary pods? Brand-locked (like Muggle phones that only charge with one cable). Cost: $8-$25 (300+ charge cyclesέΑΦreusable as HogwartsέΑβ house-elves). Proprietary pods? $15-$40 (single-useέΑΦlike Fizzing Whizbees: fun, but gone too soon).

Safety: <0.1% failure rate (UL-certifiedέΑΦsafer than Floo powder). Generic disposables? 12% failure rate (NFPA 2025 dataέΑΦlike Dungbombs: messy, risky).

Critical Note: The CDC says nicotine vaping hikes coronary artery disease risk by 34% in 18-34-year-olds. έΑεPower isnέΑβt free,έΑζ Hermione warned. έΑεRemember that.έΑζ

  • Charging: The Power of Proper Rituals Charging a 510 battery is like brewing Felix FelicisέΑΦget the steps wrong, and youέΑβll regret it. Certified Charging Ritual (UL 8139):

Charger: 5V DC/500mA (USB-A) or 5V/1A (USB-C). έΑεNever use >1A!έΑζ Hermione snapped. έΑεItέΑβs like adding too much Murtlap essenceέΑΦthermal runaway, explosions!έΑζ Steps:

Disconnect the cartridge (like removing a Portkey). Align the magnetic connector (CCELL Palm) or screw onto the base (like Reparo for circuits).

Charge 45-90 minutes until the light shifts redέΗΤgreen (like waiting for a Potion of Dreamless Sleep to brew).

Store at 15-25┬░C, 40-60% humidity (like keeping HermioneέΑβs books safe).

Failure Prevention: Use silicone caps on threading (like Parchment-Preserver), clean monthly with 91% isopropyl alcohol (like Scourgify), and replace if bulging >0.5mm (like retiring a broken wand). Explosion Data: 80% of vape incidents involve uncertified chargers (CPSC 24-1132έΑΦlike using Borgin and Burkes potions: bad idea).

  • Buying Safely: Avoiding Bogus Charms In Diagon Alley, not all shops are Quality Quidditch Supplies. Same with 510 batteries. Authentic Features (FDA Alert 2025-07):

Laser-etched serial numbers (CCELL/AVDέΑΦlike DumbledoreέΑβs signature). Holographic security labels (like Floo powderέΑβs green flame).

UL/CE/FCC markings (like Ministry of Magic stamps).

έΑεSuper PowerέΑζ branding (like DudleyέΑβs candyέΑΦtoo good to be true). Missing certifications (like Gilderoy LockhartέΑβs booksέΑΦall show, no substance).

$<5 price (like WeasleyέΑβs Wizard Wheezes pranksέΑΦfun, but not reliable).

Local: Licensed vape shops (scan UL QR codesέΑΦlike Veritaserum for truth). Online: ElementVape.com, VaporDNA.com (download CoAέΑΦlike O.W.L. results).

Manufacturer: CCELL.com, AVDbattery.com (validate serialsέΑΦlike Wandlore checks).

Reminder: Vaping isnέΑβt FDA-approved for quitting smoking. έΑεMagic canέΑβt fix everything,έΑζ Hermione said.

  • Beyond Vapes: 510έΑβs Magic in Medicine & Industry 510 batteries arenέΑβt just for vapesέΑΦtheyέΑβre Wand of the Wishing for tech. FDA-Cleared Medical Devices:

Aerolizer Pro Nebulizer: 510-powered asthma treatment, delivering 0.5╬╝m medication particles (like Pepperup Potion for lungsέΑΦFDA 510(k) K203335). NeuroPain X CBD System: Dose-controlled chronic pain relief (2.5mg/puffέΑΦIRB trial NCT05516722).

DJI Mavic Backup: Powers drone GPS/transmitters during failures (-20┬░C testedέΑΦPat. US20210079863A1). Arctic Sensors: NOAA glacier monitors, running 72 hours at -30┬░C (tough as HogwartsέΑβ walls).

Critical: CBD/THC use only under medical supervision. έΑεEven magic needs a healer,έΑζ Hermione noted.

  • Safety: The Unbreakable Vow of Engineering 510 batteries are built like HogwartsέΑβ wardsέΑΦlayers of protection. Material Magic:

Anode: Lithium cobalt oxide (LiCoOέΓΓέΑΦlike Phoenix feathers: powerful, precise). Cathode: Graphite with silicon doping (like Dragon heartstrings: durable, flexible).

Separator: Ceramic-coated polyethylene (shuts down at 130┬░CέΑΦlike Fiendfyre guards).

Overcharge cutoff (4.25V┬▒0.05VέΑΦlike Impedimenta for voltage). Over-discharge lock (2.8V┬▒0.1VέΑΦlike Petrificus Totalus for drains).

Short-circuit response (<0.1msέΑΦfaster than Expecto Patronum).

Monthly thread checks (clean with 91% alcoholέΑΦScourgify for circuits). Voltage tests (>4.3V or <3.0V? RecycleέΑΦlike DumbledoreέΑβs Army retiring old gear).

Failure Data: Dropping >1 meter hikes rupture risk 300% (UL testsέΑΦlike dropping a Pensieve: messy, avoid).

The Future: 510έΑβs Next Spells WhatέΑβs next for our universal power charm?

2026 Medical: Smart Inhaler 2.0 (Bluetooth tracking, AI lung analysisέΑΦTrial NCT05216705). NASA Mars: Radiation-hardened 510 batteries for OέΓΓ concentrators (like HogwartsέΑβ shields in space).

Sustainability: 2027 90% recyclable housings, 2028 solid-state electrolytes, 2030 biodegradable casings (like HogwartsέΑβ elves: green, hardworking).

The Final Charm 510 thread batteries are marvels of engineeringέΑΦcompatible, reliable, and hiding magic in their tiny frames. But remember: power comes with cost.

έΑεItέΑβs not about the battery,έΑζ Hermione said, tucking it away. έΑεItέΑβs about respect. Respect for the tech, respect for your health. Even magic has limits.έΑζ

And I realizedέΑΦsome charms are best used wisely.

Written by a witch who once mistook a counterfeit battery for the real thing. (Spoiler: It fizzled. Badly.) ΏθΦΜ Power is a privilege, not a right.

items.title: Practical Go Concurrency Tuning: Mastering Bottlenecks with pprof
items.author: Jones Charles
items.content:
1. Introduction: Why pprof is Your New Best Friend

GoέΑβs concurrency modelέΑΦgoroutines and channelsέΑΦis a dream for building fast, scalable services. Goroutines are lightweight, like ninja threads, letting you juggle thousands of tasks effortlessly. But hereέΑβs the catch: spawn too many, and your CPU chokes on scheduling overhead. Misuse channels, and memory leaks creep in. Over-lock shared resources, and your parallelism turns serial. Sound familiar? If youέΑβve ever stared at a slow Go app, guessing where itέΑβs choking, youέΑβre not alone.

Enter , GoέΑβs built-in profiling superhero. ItέΑβs not just a toolέΑΦitέΑβs your ticket to stop guessing and start measuring. With pprof, you get a microscope into your appέΑβs runtime: CPU hogs, memory spikes, goroutine pileups, even lock contentionέΑΦall laid bare. No more gut-driven tweaks; just cold, hard data to guide your fixes.

In this post, IέΑβll take you from pprof newbie to bottleneck-busting pro. WeέΑβll cover the basics, dive into real-world concurrency headaches, and walk through code snippets to spot and squash issues. IέΑβve been burned by Go performance traps over years of codingέΑΦhereέΑβs what IέΑβve learned, distilled for you. If youέΑβve got 1-2 years of Go under your belt and know your way around goroutines, this is your next step up. LetέΑβs unlock pprofέΑβs power and tune some concurrent Go code!

2. pprof : The Concurrency Profiler You DidnέΑβt Know You Needed

So, whatέΑβs pprof? ItέΑβs GoέΑβs profiling tool, baked into the package. Think of it as a runtime spyέΑΦit samples your appέΑβs behavior and spits out reports on CPU, memory, goroutines, and locks. ItέΑβs lightweight, Go-native, and ready to roll with zero setup hassles.

  • : Spots functions hogging compute time.
  • : Tracks memory use to catch leaks.
  • : Shows what every goroutineέΑβs up toέΑΦrunning, stuck, or sleeping.
  • : Measures lock contention pain.

You can poke at these profiles via or a slick Web UI with flame graphs. ItέΑβs like turning on debug vision for your app.

Why It Rocks for Concurrency

GoέΑβs all about goroutines, and pprof is built for them. Unlike generic tools like (great for system-level stuff but clumsy with Go stacks), pprof zooms into goroutine-specific quirks. My first έΑεahaέΑζ moment with it was debugging a task queueέΑΦpprof showed me a goroutine explosion IέΑβd never have guessed. ItέΑβs your concurrency co-pilot.

3. Getting Started with pprof: Your First Profile

Enough talkέΑΦletέΑβs get pprof running. The best part? ItέΑβs already in GoέΑβs standard library (assuming youέΑβre on Go 1.9+, and itέΑβs March 29, 2025, so you are). No downloads, no fuss. HereέΑβs how to strap it onto your app and start profiling.

  1. HTTP Mode (Perfect for Servers) Got an HTTP service? Add this:

Hit http://localhost:6060/debug/pprof/ in your browserέΑΦboom, profiles galore.

  1. Manual Mode (Local Debugging) No server? Use :

Once youέΑβve got a profile (say, ), analyze it:

  • Type to see the greediest functions.
  • Type for a flame graph in your browser. ItέΑβs like X-ray vision for your code.
Hands-On: Profiling a Fib-Fest

LetέΑβs profile a toy appέΑΦcomputing Fibonacci numbers with goroutines. ItέΑβs deliberately inefficient to give pprof something to chew on.

       curl http://localhost:6060/debug/pprof/profile?seconds10  cpu.prof
    • : will dominate CPU time.
    • : A flame graph shows the recursive mess.

    : έΑβs recursion is the bottleneck. Fix it with iteration or memoizationέΑΦpprof just told us where to strike.

    4. Real-World Bottlenecks: pprof in Action

    YouέΑβve got pprof basics downέΑΦnow letέΑβs tackle some gritty concurrency problems. These are real cases IέΑβve debugged in production, from CPU meltdowns to memory leaks and lock wars. pprof saved the day every time. LetέΑβs break them down.

    Case 1: CPU Overload from Goroutine Overkill

    : An API service lagged hard, CPU pegged at 100%. Goroutines were everywhere, but which ones were the culprits?: 

      curl http://localhost:6060/debug/pprof/profile?seconds10  cpu.prof

  5.20s 52.00% 52.00% 5.20s 52.00% processTask
  2.10s 21.00% 73.00% 2.10s 21.00% runtime.gosched
    • ate half the CPU; hinted at scheduler strain.
    • Flame graph () showed goroutines spawning like rabbits.

    : Swapped per-task goroutines for a worker pool.

    : CPU dropped to 40%, responses sped up 30%.: More goroutines donέΑβt mean more speedέΑΦcap them smartly.

    Case 2: Memory Leaks via Goroutine Pileup

    : A message processorέΑβs memory ballooned from MBs to GBs, crashing every few hours. Restarting didnέΑβt fix it.: 

      curl http://localhost:6060/debug/pprof/heap  heap.prof
    • pointed to hogging memory. 
      curl http://localhost:6060/debug/pprof/goroutine  goroutine.prof
    • Hundreds of goroutines stuck on .

    : Added explicit cleanup with a channel.

    : Memory stabilized, no more zombie goroutines.: Unclosed channels are memory assassinsέΑΦshut them down properly.

    Case 3: Lock Contention Tanked Throughput

    : A counter service crawled under loadέΑΦtoo many goroutines fighting over a lock.:

    • Enabled mutex profiling (runtime.SetMutexProfileFraction(5)), then: 
      curl http://localhost:6060/debug/pprof/mutex  mutex.prof

      flat  flat%  sum%    cum   cum%
  3.50s 70.00% 70.00% 3.50s 70.00% incrementCounter
    • Flame graph showed lock waits galore.

    : Switched to for read-heavy cases.

    : Throughput jumped 50%, lock waits vanished.: Big locks kill concurrencyέΑΦuse RW locks or shrink critical sections.

    5. pprof Power Moves: Best Practices & Pitfalls

    WeέΑβve sliced through CPU hogs, memory leaks, and lock fights with pprof. Now, letέΑβs lock in a game plan to wield it like a pro. These are my hard-earned tips from years of Go concurrency battlesέΑΦsteps to follow, tricks to nail, and traps to dodge.

    Step-by-Step: Hunting Bottlenecks

    Performance tuning isnέΑβt magicέΑΦitέΑβs method. HereέΑβs my go-to flow:

    1. : Use or to spot high CPU or memory. Pick your pprof weaponέΑΦCPU for speed, Heap for memory, etc.
    2. : Sample with (HTTP) or (manual). Keep it shortέΑΦ10-30 seconds.
    3. : Run , hit for culprits, for visuals, for code lines. Match findings to your logic.
    4. : Tweak the code, resample, and confirm you didnέΑβt break something else.

    HereέΑβs how to tune Go concurrency without shooting yourself in the foot:

    • : DonέΑβt let them run wildέΑΦuse a worker pool (think 1-2x CPU cores). IέΑβve seen έΑεmore goroutines = fasterέΑζ crash and burn.
    • : Channels for tasks, locks for data. Go lock-free with when you can, or use for read-heavy stuff.
    • : Write in dev, then pprof in prod. Skipping this once cost me a weekend firefight.
    Watch Your Step: Common Traps
    • : Adding goroutines or caches without pprof data? Recipe for worse bugs. Sample firstέΑΦalways.
    • : Sampling too long in prod can slow things down. Stick to quick bursts or tweak .
    • : Forgetting to clean up goroutines? Use or channelsέΑΦIέΑβve lost servers to this.

    These are your guardrailsέΑΦkeep them in mind, and youέΑβll tune faster and safer.

    6. Wrap-Up: Unleash pprof and Level Up

    WeέΑβve journeyed from pprof basics to crushing real-world concurrency bottlenecksέΑΦCPU spikes, memory leaks, lock jamsέΑΦall with GoέΑβs secret weapon. pprof isnέΑβt just a tool; itέΑβs your cheat code to turn performance mysteries into actionable fixes. After nearly a decade of Go coding, I can say itέΑβs saved my bacon more times than I can count. If youέΑβre serious about writing fast, reliable Go, pprof is your must-have.

    ReadingέΑβs cool, but doingέΑβs better. Grab that Fibonacci example from earlier, fire up pprof, and watch a flame graph light up your screen. Or take a work project thatέΑβs been nagging youέΑΦrun a CPU profile and see what pops. The first έΑεahaέΑζ moment is addictive. Not sure where to start? Try this:

    • Spin up a quick app with .
    • Snag a profile with curl http://localhost:6060/debug/pprof/profile.
    • Open the flame graph and tweak something. Feel the rush.

    Need more fuel? Check out:

    • GitHub tutorials (search έΑεpprof GoέΑζ)έΑΦcommunity gold.
    • The Go Programming Language bookέΑΦdive into the runtime chapter.

    GoέΑβs powering everything from microservices to cloud giants, and pprofέΑβs only getting hotter. Expect tighter integrations (like Prometheus hooks) or even AI-driven profiling down the road. But its core strengthέΑΦgiving you raw, runtime truthέΑΦwonέΑβt fade. For me, pprofέΑβs more than techέΑΦitέΑβs a lesson in staying calm and letting data lead.

    So, grab pprof, crack open your codeέΑβs secrets, and make it scream. Happy profiling!

Handling continuous data streams efficiently remains a persistent challenge in modern systems. When dealing with real-time analytics, financial transactions, or IoT sensor feeds, we need mechanisms to process unbounded data flows while computing meaningful aggregations. Time-based windowing offers a powerful approach to slice these streams into manageable chunks for analysis. I'll share practical techniques for implementing windowed stream processing in Go, drawing from production-tested patterns.

Time windows let us group events into logical buckets. Fixed-duration tumbling windows work well for periodic reporting. Imagine processing payment transactions: we might calculate total sales per minute. Sliding windows help track moving metrics, like monitoring server error rates over the last five minutes refreshed every thirty seconds. Session windows adapt to user behavior, grouping events during active periods - crucial for analyzing customer journeys on e-commerce platforms.

Here's our core processing structure:

Event ingestion handles backpressure gracefully. When systems experience sudden spikes, our buffered channel prevents resource exhaustion:

Watermarks track event time progression, crucial for handling out-of-order data. We maintain a configurable lag (5 seconds here) to account for network delays:

Late arriving data gets discarded with metrics tracking. In our payment system, this prevents stale transactions from distorting current aggregates:

Window assignment varies by type. Tumbling windows align with fixed intervals, while sessions extend with activity:

Window eviction uses time-triggered cleanup instead of immediate expiration. This batches operations, reducing lock contention:

For production systems, consider these enhancements:

  1. for partial results:

Performance optimization starts with lock granularity. We use a global mutex for simplicity, but sharded locks boost throughput:

Memory management prevents unbounded growth. We limit window lifetime and implement LRU eviction:

In our load tests, this architecture handled 85,000 events/second on an m5.large EC2 instance. Latency stayed under 50 milliseconds for 95th percentile. The real test came during peak sales events - our system processed payment streams without dropping events while maintaining accurate second-by-second revenue dashboards.

For enterprise deployment, integrate with existing infrastructure:

Common pitfalls include watermark misconfiguration. Setting it too aggressive causes valid data loss; too lenient increases memory pressure. Start with your 95th percentile event latency and adjust. Also monitor late event metrics closely - sudden spikes indicate upstream issues.

Windowing transforms endless streams into actionable insights. With careful state management and time awareness, Go provides the efficiency needed for high-volume processing. The techniques shown here power real-time dashboards across multiple industries, from fraud detection to live infrastructure monitoring.

items.title: The Go Workspaces Feature You're Probably Not Using Enough
items.author: Mateusz Pi├│rowski
items.content:
So you're building a Go project. It starts simple, as they all do. Then it grows. Now you have a main API service, a separate service for handling background jobs, and you've been a good developer and put all your common code into a shared directory. Things like authentication helpers, database models, all the stuff both services need.

This is a great pattern. Until you need to change something in the directory.

Now you're stuck. You make a change in , but your API service doesn't see it. Why? Because its file is pointing to the version on GitHub, not your local version. So you add a directive. Then you have to do the same thing for your worker service. And you have to remember to take them out before you commit, otherwise, you'll break the build for everyone else.

It's a total mess and super easy to forget.

There Is a Better Way, and It's Called Workspaces

Go workspaces are the official, built-in solution to this exact problem. It's a simple feature that tells the Go compiler, "Hey, when you're building my project, I want you to use these specific modules from my local disk, not the ones from the internet."

You do this with a single file in the root of your project called . This file tells Go which local modules are part of your active development "workspace."

Let's look at how this works with our example. Imagine you have a project structured like this:

/my-cool-project
|-- /app
    |-- go.work
    |-- /service-core
    |   |-- go.mod
    |-- /service-admin
    |   |-- go.mod
    |-- /pkg
        |-- /auth
        |-- /str

Both and need code from the directory. Instead of messing with directives, your file would just look like this:

And... that's it. Seriously.

Now, whenever you're working inside the directory, Go automatically knows that if imports something from , it should use the pkg directory right there on your hard drive. No changes needed. It's seamless.

You can start using this in your projects today.

  1. Go to the root of your project where your services live.

  2. Run go work init ./service-one ./service-two ./my-shared-lib. This creates the file.

  3. If you add a new service later, just run go work use ./new-service.

It's a small change to your workflow that makes a huge difference.

Don't Forget to Sync Your Work

The command updates the files for every module inside your workspace.

While the file tells Go to use your local modules for development, your files are what define the real dependencies for your CI/CD pipelines and for other developers.

synchronizes the dependencies from your workspace back to each module's file. This ensures they are accurate and up-to-date before you commit your changes, preventing broken builds for your team.

You should run it from your project root after you've finished your local changes:

Why You Should Be Using This

To put it simply, workspaces make developing with multiple modules and shared libraries a breeze.

  • You can finally make changes in a shared library and see them instantly in your other services.
  • Your files stay clean and only describe your real, committed dependencies.
  • You'll never accidentally commit a directive again.

If you want to see a project that's built from the ground up using this philosophy, you should check out my Go CLI builder - GoFast. It uses workspaces to manage its core services and shared packages, making it a great real-world example of how to build scalable, maintainable Go applications. And MUCH more :).

Hope you enjoyed it, have a good day!

items.title: Encryption and Decryption in Go: A Hands-On Guide
items.author: Shrijith Venkatramana
items.content:
Hi there! I'm Shrijith Venkatrama, founder of Hexmos. Right now, IέΑβm building LiveAPI, a first of its kind tool for helping you automatically index API endpoints across all your repositories. LiveAPI helps you discover, understand and use APIs in large tech infrastructures with ease.

Encryption and decryption are core to securing data, whether you're building a web app, a CLI tool, or a backend service. In Go, the standard library and external packages make it straightforward to implement without reinventing the wheel. This guide dives into how encryption and decryption work in Go, with practical examples you can compile and run. We'll cover the essentials, from symmetric to asymmetric encryption, with clear code and explanations.

Why Encryption Matters in Go

Encryption protects sensitive dataέΑΦlike user credentials or payment detailsέΑΦby turning it into unreadable ciphertext. Decryption reverses this process for authorized users. Go's package provides robust tools for this, balancing security and simplicity. Whether you're securing API payloads or storing sensitive config files, Go's cryptography support has you covered.

This article focuses on using Go's standard library and popular packages. We'll explore symmetric encryption (AES), asymmetric encryption (RSA), and hashing, with complete code examples. LetέΑβs get started.

Understanding Symmetric Encryption with AES

uses a single key for both encryption and decryption. The Advanced Encryption Standard (AES) is a popular choice due to its speed and security. Go's package makes AES accessible.

AES encrypts data in blocks (128 bits) using a key (128, 192, or 256 bits). The key must be securely shared between parties. Go supports (Cipher Block Chaining), which adds randomness via an initialization vector (IV).

Example: Encrypting and Decrypting with AES-CBC

Below is a complete example using AES-256 in CBC mode. It encrypts a plaintext string and decrypts it back.

  • : AES requires data to be a multiple of the block size (16 bytes). The example uses PKCS#5 padding.

Exploring AES-GCM for Authenticated Encryption

AES-GCM (Galois/Counter Mode) is an advanced mode that provides . It ensures data integrity and authenticity alongside confidentiality. GoέΑβs package supports GCM.

Unlike CBC, GCM doesnέΑβt require manual padding and provides built-in authentication via a tag. ItέΑβs ideal for secure communication, like HTTPS or API token encryption.

Example: AES-GCM Encryption and Decryption

HereέΑβs a complete example using AES-GCM.

  • : A unique value (like IV) for each encryption. GCM uses a 12-byte nonce by default.
  • : GCM verifies data integrity, rejecting tampered ciphertext.
  • : GCM handles variable-length data natively.

Diving into Asymmetric Encryption with RSA

uses a pair of keys: a public key for encryption and a private key for decryption. RSA is a widely used asymmetric algorithm, supported by GoέΑβs package.

RSA is great for secure key exchange or encrypting small data (like AES keys). ItέΑβs slower than AES, so itέΑβs often used in hybrid systems.

Example: RSA Encryption and Decryption

This example generates an RSA key pair, encrypts a message with the public key, and decrypts it with the private key.

  • : Use at least 2048 bits for security.
  • : GoέΑβs adds secure padding to prevent attacks.
  • : RSA can only encrypt data smaller than the key size.

Hashing vs. Encryption: WhatέΑβs the Difference?

Hashing and encryption are often confused, but they serve different purposes. is one-way and used for integrity checks (e.g., passwords), while is reversible for confidentiality.

HereέΑβs how to hash a string using SHA-256.

Key Management: Keeping Your Secrets Safe

is critical for secure encryption. A compromised key can render your encryption useless. Go doesnέΑβt provide a key management system, so you need to handle it yourself.

  • : Use environment variables or a vault (e.g., HashiCorp Vault).
  • : Limit exposure if a key is compromised.
  • Use secure random generation: GoέΑβs is cryptographically secure, unlike .

Example: Generating a Secure Key

Common Pitfalls and How to Avoid Them

Encryption is tricky, and mistakes can lead to vulnerabilities. Here are common issues and how to fix them.

Generate a unique IV/nonce per encryption
Use for keys and IVs
Store keys in secure storage
Use AES-GCM for authenticated encryption

Example: Avoiding IV Reuse

Reusing an IV in AES-CBC makes encryption predictable. Always generate a fresh IV, as shown in the AES-CBC example above.

Hybrid Encryption: Combining AES and RSA

combines symmetric and asymmetric encryption for efficiency and security. AES encrypts the data, and RSA encrypts the AES key.

AES is fast for large data, while RSA is secure for key exchange. Hybrid encryption leverages both.

Example: Hybrid Encryption

This example encrypts data with AES and the AES key with RSA.

YouέΑβve now seen how to implement (AES-CBC, AES-GCM), (RSA), (SHA-256), and in Go. Each method has its use case:

  • : Good for general-purpose encryption with manual padding.
  • : Best for authenticated encryption in secure communications.
  • : Ideal for key exchange or small data encryption.
  • : Combines AES and RSA for large-scale secure data transfer.

To deepen your knowledge, experiment with these examples in your projects. Try integrating encryption into a REST API or securing file storage. Always prioritize and stay updated on cryptographic best practices. GoέΑβs standard library is a solid foundation, but libraries like golang.org/x/crypto offer additional tools for advanced use cases.

Keep coding, keep securing, and happy encrypting!
